688.name: Fluvastatin (Dosage)
688.description: Fluvastatin is a drug of the statin family used to lower blood cholesterol levels and prevent cardiovascular disease. The efficacy and safety of fluvastatin may be influenced by genetic variants in the SLCO1B1 gene and in the cytochrome CYP2C9. These variants may affect the pharmacokinetics of the drug and increase the risk of developing musculoskeletal symptoms associated with its use.
688.overview:
Statins are drugs that act as inhibitors of the enzyme hydroxymethylglutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) reductase, which plays a key role in cholesterol synthesis. These drugs have a wide range of beneficial effects, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antiproliferative and immunomodulatory properties. In addition, they help to maintain the stability of arterial plaques and prevent platelet aggregation. Fluvastatin is primarily used to treat hypercholesterolemia and, when combined with proper diet and exercise, may reduce the risk of heart attack or stroke in people at risk for heart disease.

Fluvastatin was the first fully synthetic statin, patented in 1982. It is on the World Health Organization's list of essential medicines. It is a widely used drug because of its safety profile in both adults and children.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Statins are inhibitors of 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, a key enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis. As a consequence, SREBP(sterol regulatory elements-binding proteins) regulatory proteins are activated, which increase the number of LDL cell receptors, decreasing circulating cholesterol levels.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Flavastatin is contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to this drug or to any of its excipients. In addition, it should not be prescribed in people with hepatic problems, pregnancy or together with cyclosporine. Furthermore, its use is not recommended in pregnant women.

In addition, it should be used with caution in elderly patients (>70 years), people with family or personal history of muscular disorders, hypothyroidism, alcoholism or renal insufficiency.

Unlike other statins, its metabolism involves several liver enzymes (mainly CYP2C9, but also others), which makes it less susceptible to interactions than other drugs of the same family.

SIDE EFFECTS

The use of fluvastatin shares adverse effects with other drugs of the same family. It can cause headaches, constipation, diarrhea, dyspepsia or nausea. Other infrequent and rare adverse effects are anemia, insomnia, dizziness, abdominal pain, urticaria, fatigue or edema. Myopathy is another of the most common adverse effects, consisting of muscle pain, weakness and increased muscle enzymes. Identification of this statin-induced myopathy is not easy and resolves after drug withdrawal.

A rare but significant side effect is rhabdomyolysis, a potentially serious condition that tends to occur more frequently when combined with other drugs, especially fibrates.

BRAND NAMES

Drugs containing lovastatin as an active ingredient include:

Lescol®
Fluindostatin®
Canef®
Cranoc®
Vastin®
688.dose: The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) guideline suggests recommendations on fluvastatin dosing according to SLCO1B1 and CYP2C9 genotypes. Specifically, for carriers of a reduced function allele, it is recommended to start therapy with standard doses, monitoring patients taking more than 40mg for possible myopathy risk. For carriers of two copies of a reduced function SLCO1B1 or CYP2C9 intermediate metabolizers, it is recommended not to exceed a dose of 40mg or to consider an alternative drug. In the case of CYP2C9 poor metabolizers, it is recommended not to exceed 20mg or to use an alternative statin. Finally, for carriers of a loss of function SLCOB1 allele who are also deficient metabolizers for CYP2C9, it is recommended to use an alternative drug.
688.technical_description:
Fluvastatin is an inhibitor of the enzyme 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, a crucial enzyme in the cholesterol synthesis pathway in the liver. By blocking this enzyme, the conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate is reduced, which decreases endogenous cholesterol production and thus blood cholesterol concentrations. This mechanism also causes an increase in the expression of LDL receptors in hepatocytes and stimulates the degradation of circulating LDL. In this way, low-density lipoprotein (LDL, commonly known as bad cholesterol) levels decrease and high-density lipoprotein (HDL, commonly known as good cholesterol) levels increase. Together, these effects help reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease by improving the patient's lipid profile.

The metabolism of fluvastatin involves several genes that regulate both its metabolism and its transport in the liver. Fluvastatin undergoes a hydroxylation process in which the main enzyme responsible for the formation of its hydroxylated metabolites is CYP2C9. Therefore, genetic variations that decrease the activity of this cytochrome can increase the bioavailability of fluvastatin, making the appearance of adverse effects more probable.

On the other hand, the SLCO1B1 gene encodes transporter proteins that facilitate the transport of the drug from the blood to the hepatocytes. Genetic variations in this transporter alter systemic exposure to statins, which may increase the risk of myopathy. Specifically, the presence of certain polymorphisms decreases the activity of the transporter, increasing the levels of statins, the presumed causal factor of myopathy induced by these drugs.

689.name: Lovastatin (Dosage)
689.description: Lovastatin, as an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor, lowers cholesterol levels by blocking its synthesis in the liver. Its metabolism, mainly regulated by CYP3A4 enzymes and the transporter protein SLCO1B1, can vary between individuals in the presence of certain genetic changes. Muscle myopathy is one of the main adverse effects of statin treatment, and genomic variants have been described that increase the predisposition to suffer it.
689.dose: The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) guideline suggests recommendations for lovastatin dosing based on SLCO1B1 genotypes. Specifically, for carriers of a reduced-function allele, it is recommended to use an alternative statin or a dose lower than 20mg. For carriers of two copies, it is recommended not to use this drug and to use an alternative statin or lipid-lowering drug.
689.technical_description:
Lovastatin is an inhibitor of the enzyme 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, a crucial enzyme in the cholesterol synthesis pathway in the liver. By blocking this enzyme, the conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate is reduced, which lowers blood cholesterol concentration. This mechanism also causes an increase in the expression of LDL (low-density lipoprotein, commonly known as bad cholesterol) receptors in hepatocytes and stimulates the degradation of circulating LDL. Together, these effects help reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease by improving the patient's lipid profile.

Lovastatin is administered as an inactive prodrug in lactone form, which is reversibly converted to its active form, lovastatinic acid, in plasma, liver and intestinal mucosa. The oxidative metabolism of lovastatin is mediated mainly by the cytochrome CYP3A4 enzyme. The SLCO1B1 gene, which expresses a transporter protein (OATP1B1) that facilitates the transport of lovastatin from the blood to the hepatocytes, is also involved in the metabolism of this drug.

Genetic variability in the SLCO1B1 gene has been associated with the pharmacokinetics and adverse effects of lovastatin by altering systemic exposure to statins, which may increase the risk of myopathy. Specifically, the presence of certain polymorphisms decreases the activity of the transporter, increasing the levels of statins, the presumed causal factor of myopathy induced by these drugs.

690.name: Pitavastatin (Dosage)
690.description: Pitavastatin is a drug of the statin family used to lower blood cholesterol levels and prevent cardiovascular disease. Muscle myopathy is one of the main adverse effects of statin treatment, and genomic variants have been described that increase the predisposition to suffer it.
690.overview:
Statins are drugs that act as inhibitors of the enzyme hydroxymethylglutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) reductase, which plays a key role in cholesterol synthesis. These drugs have a wide range of beneficial effects, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antiproliferative and immunomodulatory properties. They also help to maintain the stability of arterial plaques and prevent platelet aggregation.

Pitavastatin has been marketed since 2005 and has been shown to have the highest bioavailability rate of all the statins marketed to date, which allows it to be used at lower doses. In addition, it has few drug-drug interactions, minimizing the risk of adverse effects in polymedicated patients. It is approved by the FDA to treat heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia in patients over 8 years of age and as adjunctive therapy for primary and mixed dyslipidemia. Unlike other statins, it has not been clearly established whether pitavastatin offers additional benefits in reducing cardiovascular problems.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Statins are inhibitors of 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, a key enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis. As a consequence, SREBP(sterol regulatory elements-binding proteins) regulatory proteins are activated, which increase the number of LDL cell receptors, decreasing circulating cholesterol levels.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Pitavastatin is contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to this drug or to any of its excipients. In addition, it should not be prescribed in people with hepatic problems, pregnancy or together with cyclosporine. In addition, its use is not recommended in pregnant women.

In addition, it should be used with caution in elderly patients (>70 years), people with a family or personal history of muscular disorders, hypothyroidism, alcoholism or renal insufficiency.

SIDE EFFECTS

Adverse effects of pitavastatin are generally similar to those of other statins and include headaches, constipation, diarrhea, dyspepsia or nausea. Other infrequent and rare adverse effects include anemia, insomnia, dizziness, abdominal pain, urticaria, fatigue or edema. Myopathy is another of the most common adverse effects, consisting of muscle pain, weakness and increased muscle enzymes. Identification of this statin-induced myopathy is not easy and resolves after withdrawal of the drug.

BRAND NAMES

Drugs containing lovastatin as an active ingredient include:

Livalo®
Zypitamag®
Nikita®
690.dose: The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) guideline suggests recommendations for pitavastatin dosing based on SLCO1B1 genotypes. Specifically, for carriers of a reduced function allele, it is recommended to use a pitavastatin dose lower than 2 mg, and if the desired therapeutic effect is not achieved, consider an alternative statin. For carriers of two copies, it is recommended not to exceed a dose of 1 mg or consider an alternative drug.
690.technical_description:
Pitavastatin is an inhibitor of the enzyme 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, a crucial enzyme in the cholesterol synthesis pathway in the liver. By blocking this enzyme, the conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate is reduced, which lowers blood cholesterol concentration. This mechanism also causes an increase in the expression of LDL (low-density lipoprotein, commonly known as bad cholesterol) receptors in hepatocytes and stimulates the degradation of circulating LDL. Together, these effects help reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease by improving the patient's lipid profile.

Most statins are metabolized through one or more cytochrome P450 liver enzymes, which may increase unwanted drug interactions. However, pitavastatin is metabolized primarily through glucuronidation, making it a useful drug in patients requiring multiple medications.

The metabolism of this drug also involves the SLCO1B1 gene, which expresses a transporter protein (OATP1B1) that facilitates the transport of pitavastatin from the blood to the hepatocytes. Genetic variability in the SLCO1B1 gene has been associated with the pharmacokinetics and adverse effects of pitavastatin, which may increase the risk of myopathy. Specifically, the presence of certain polymorphisms decreases the activity of the transporter, increasing the levels of statins, the presumed causal factor of myopathy induced by these drugs.

691.name: Rosuvastatin (Dosage)
691.description: Rosuvastatin is a drug of the statin family used to lower blood cholesterol levels and prevent cardiovascular disease. Muscle myopathy is one of the main adverse effects of statin treatment, and genomic variants have been described that increase the predisposition to suffer it.
691.overview:
Statins are drugs that act as inhibitors of the enzyme hydroxymethylglutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) reductase, which plays a key role in cholesterol synthesis. These drugs have a wide range of beneficial effects, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antiproliferative and immunomodulatory properties. They also help to maintain the stability of arterial plaques and prevent platelet aggregation.

Rosuvastatin has been approved by the FDA to treat homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, hyperlipidemia, mixed dyslipidemia, primary dysbetalipoproteinemia, hypertriglyceridemia and prevention of cardiovascular disease.

Rosuvastatin is of synthetic origin, patented in 1991. In 2021, it was among the 30 most prescribed drugs. Its safety profile allows its use both in adults and in children over 8 years of age with hypercholesterolemia of genetic origin.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Statins are inhibitors of 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, a key enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis. As a consequence, SREBP(sterol regulatory elements-binding proteins) regulatory proteins are activated, which increase the number of LDL cell receptors, decreasing circulating cholesterol levels.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Rosuvastatin is contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to this drug or to any of its excipients. In addition, it should not be prescribed in people with hepatic problems, with renal insufficiency, concomitant to treatment with cyclosporines or during pregnancy.

In addition, it should be used with caution in elderly patients (>70 years), people with family or personal history of muscular disorders, hypothyroidism, alcoholism or renal insufficiency.

It is important to bear in mind that the simultaneous use of rosuvastatin with other drugs can alter the bioavailability of this statin, increasing the risk of undesirable adverse effects. Among the drugs that have shown interaction are cyclosporine, protease inhibitors (ritornavir/atazanavir), regorafenib, some antibiotics such as erythromycin or antacids, among others. Therefore, it is important to inform the physician of any prescription or non-prescription treatment taken simultaneously with rosuvastatin.

SIDE EFFECTS

The use of rosuvastatin shares adverse effects with other drugs of the same family. It may cause headaches, constipation, diarrhea, dyspepsia, nausea or asthenia. Other infrequent and rare adverse effects are urticaria, increased transaminases, jaundice or edema. Myopathy is another of the most common adverse effects, consisting of muscle pain, weakness and increased muscle enzymes. The identification of this statin-induced myopathy is not easy and resolves after withdrawal of the drug.

BRAND NAMES

Drugs containing lovastatin as an active ingredient include:

Crestor®
Ezallor®
Roszet®
Rosumed®
Rosvel®
691.dose: The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) guideline suggests recommendations for fluvastatin dosing according to SLCO1B1 and CYP2C9 genotypes. Specifically, for carriers of a reduced-function allele, it is recommended to initiate therapy with standard doses, monitoring patients taking more than 20mg for potential myopathy risk. For carriers of two reduced-function copies of SLCO1B1 or ABCG2, it is recommended not to exceed a dose of 20mg or to consider an alternative drug. In the case of carriers of two reduced-function copies of SLCO1B1 together with two reduced-function copies of ABCG2, it is recommended not to exceed 10mg or to use an alternative statin.
691.technical_description:
Rosuvastatin acts as an inhibitor of the enzyme 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, a key component in the cholesterol synthesis pathway in the liver. By blocking this enzyme, the conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate is reduced, leading to a decrease in blood cholesterol concentration. In addition, this mechanism increases the expression of LDL receptors (low density lipoproteins, known as "bad" cholesterol) in hepatocytes, promoting the degradation of circulating LDL. These combined effects help reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease by improving the patient's lipid profile. In addition, rosuvastatin exhibits additional beneficial properties, such as inhibition of platelet aggregation, anticoagulant effects, reduction of inflammation in coronary plaque, and improvement of endothelial function.

The SLCO1B1 gene encodes for a hepatic transporter that facilitates the uptake of these drugs. Genetic variations in this gene alter systemic exposure to statins, which may increase the risk of myopathy. Specifically, the presence of certain polymorphisms decreases the activity of the transporter, increasing the levels of statins, the presumed causal factor of myopathy induced by these drugs.

The ABCG2 gene codes for a transporter that modulates the absorption and disposition of rosuvastatin. The c.421C>A variant (rs2231142) has been extensively studied for its relationship with the plasma bioavailability of rosuvastatin. Specifically, carriers of the minority T allele show a worse expression of the transporter and higher levels of this statin, which is related to an increased risk of myopathy.
691.technical_citations:
Tuteja S, Rader DJ. SLCO1B1 and Statin Therapy. Circ Genom Precis Med. 2018 Sep;11(9):e002320.

Cooper-DeHoff RM. The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline for SLCO1B1, ABCG2, and CYP2C9 genotypes and Statin-Associated Musculoskeletal Symptoms. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2022 May;111(5):1007-1021.

 Luvai A, Mbagaya W, Hall AS, et al. Rosuvastatin: a review of the pharmacology and clinical effectiveness in cardiovascular disease. Clin Med Insights Cardiol. 2012;6:17-33.

692.name: G6PD Deficiency and Adverse Drug Reactions
692.description: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, also known as favism, is the most common enzyme deficiency in the world, caused by the presence of mutations in the G6PD gene. It is characterized by episodes of hemolysis that can be triggered by certain factors, including many drugs.
692.overview:
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is a metabolic disorder in which the destruction of red blood cells (hemolysis) occurs when a person is exposed to certain triggers such as drugs, foods (such as fava beans), or stressful situations or infection.

It is an X-linked inherited disease, so it is more common in men, who may also have a more severe phenotype. It is important to note that heterozygous women may show some symptoms due to the X chromosome inactivation process.

In general, the disease does not produce symptoms until the body is exposed to the triggers.

The most common symptoms of the hemolytic episode are:

Dark-colored urineFeverAbdominal painFatiguePalenessRapid heart rateJaundiceRegarding the treatment of G6PD deficiency, there is no specific treatment for the disease itself, but episodes of hemolysis can be effectively managed by discontinuing the triggering drug or food. In more severe, although rare, cases, a blood transfusion may be necessary.

Therefore, it is important that carriers of some variant of predisposition to G6PD deficiency are aware of the triggering agents of hemolysis episodes and avoid their consumption.

Regulatory agencies around the world advise against the use of certain medications in people with G6PD deficiency. It is important to note that exposure to certain drugs can trigger episodes of hemolysis in people with G6PD deficiency. The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) guideline recommends avoiding the consumption of the following medications and compounds due to the high risk of hemolysis:

Dapsone: antibiotic used to treat leprosy and some skin infections.Methylene bluePegloticaseRasburicaseTafenoquinePrimaquineNitrofurantoinToluidine blueThere are other compounds that should be used with caution, as they may increase the risk of hemolysis in people with G6PD deficiency. In addition to the above drugs, there are many others in which special caution should be taken due to the increased risk of hemolysis in people carrying G6PD deficiency. These include: sulfoxones, ascorbic acid, bupivacaine, ciprofloxacin, diethylamine, dimenhydrinate, dimercaprol, dorzolamide, enoxacin, furalidone, gliclazide, glimepiride, hydroxychloroquine, nitric oxide, acetylsalicylic acid, or sulfamethoxazole, among others.

It is essential that patients with G6PD deficiency are aware of the risks associated with certain medications, especially those used to treat malaria, such as primaquine and tafenoquine. These medications are specifically contraindicated in people with G6PD deficiency due to their ability to trigger hemolysis and endanger the patient's health. Ensuring that the medications used do not interfere with red blood cell function is essential to avoid serious complications.
692.technical_description:
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is associated with the development of acute hemolytic anemia in the context of oxidative stress, which may be caused by drug exposure.

The G6PD gene codes for an enzyme that converts glucose-6-phosphate to 6-phosphogluconolactone and is the first step in the pentose phosphate pathway. This metabolic pathway is especially important in erythrocytes, as it protects them from the oxidative stress to which they are highly exposed. Erythrocytes deficient in G6PD are more susceptible to drug-induced lysis. The phenotype that may be triggered is dependent on the type of mutation and the presence of one or two copies of the mutation, in addition to other factors.

To date, about 150 different alleles of the G6PD gene have been reported. Most of the variants are "nonsense" resulting in an amino acid change or deletions. These alleles have been classified into different types by the WHO as follows: class A (20% enzyme activity/chronic hemolysis), class B (<45% activity/acute triggered hemolysis), class C (60-100% activity/no hemolysis) and class U (any activity without clinical significance).

Being an X-linked gene, in females, a combination of genetic and biochemical tests is necessary to diagnose the deficiency as it is difficult to estimate the impact of a heterozygous variant due to X-chromosome mosaicism. It is estimated that almost 5% of the world's population is G6PD deficient, and since heterozygosity is associated with protection against malaria, the frequency of carriers in endemic countries in Asia and Africa is higher, reaching up to 30%.
692.technical_citations:
Tuteja S, Rader DJ. Expanded Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline for Medication Use in the Context of G6PD Genotype. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2023 May;113(5):973-985.

Nannelli C. et al. Genetic variants causing G6PD deficiency: Clinical and biochemical data support new WHO classification. Br J Haematol. 2023 Sep;202(5):1024-1032.

Richardson SR and O´Malley GF. Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-.

693.name: Vortioxetine (Dosage)
693.description: Vortioxetine is an antidepressant that acts as a serotonin and noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor and modulates several serotonergic receptors. This drug improves neuronal transmission and has positive effects on cognitive symptoms of depression. Metabolization of vortioxetine mainly involves the CYP2D6 enzyme, and genetic variants in this gene can affect the plasma concentration of the drug.
693.overview:
Vortioxetine is an antidepressant that acts as a serotonin and noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor and modulates serotonergic receptors. This drug was licensed by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat depression in 2013.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Its mechanism of action is multimodal. On the one hand, it increases the concentration of the neurotransmitters serotonin and noradrenaline in the synaptic cleft by blocking their reuptake, thus enhancing their transmission. On the other hand, it modulates postsynaptic serotonin receptors, either activating or blocking them.

Vortioxetine can influence the neurotransmission of several systems, such as serotonin, noradrenaline, dopamine, acetylcholine, histamine, glutamate and gamma-aminobutyric acid. This multimodal action, affecting different complex neuronal networks, may explain the improved efficacy in the treatment of cognitive symptoms of depression.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Vortioxetine should not be used in patients with known hypersensitivity to this active ingredient or to any of its excipients. Concomitant use with selective or non-selective monoamine oxidase inhibitors is also not recommended due to the risk of serotonin syndrome.

Special caution is recommended in combination therapy with migraine medications such as triptans, other antidepressants and neuroleptics, or CYP2D6 inhibitors such as bupropion. Some supplements such as St. John's wort may also cause unwanted effects.

SIDE EFFECTS

The most common adverse effects of vortioxetine include nausea, dry mouth, dizziness, gastrointestinal problems such as constipation or diarrhea, sexual dysfunction, and vomiting.

BRAND NAMES

Trintellix®
Brintellix®
693.dose: The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) clinical guideline for vortioxetine suggests recommendations in the dosing of these drugs according to the CYP2D6 cytochrome metabolizer status. For CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizers, it is recommended to select an alternative drug not predominantly metabolized by CYP2D6. For poor metabolizers, start with 50% of the initial dose and titrate up to the maximum recommended dose of 10 mg or consider an alternative antidepressant not predominantly metabolized by CYP2D6.
693.technical_description:
Vortioxetine is an antidepressant with two pharmacological targets: serotonin receptors and transporters. Vortioxetine exerts as a 5-HT1A receptor agonist, 5-HT3, 5-HT7 and 5-HT1D receptor antagonist, 5-HT1B receptor partial agonist and 5-HT transporter (SERT) inhibitor.

Vortioxetine undergoes a metabolization process, mainly through oxidation mediated by cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes, followed by glucuronan conjugation via uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase. Cytochrome P450 enzymes responsible for the biotransformation of the parent compound include CYP2D6, CYP3A4/5, CYP2A6, CYP2C9 and CYP2C19, with a prominent role of CYP2D6 as the predominant enzyme in this process.

Since genetic variants in the CYP2D6 gene can alter vortioxetine blood levels, the CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) guideline recommends adjusting the initial dose of this drug based on genotypes. This adjustment aims to minimize the risk of adverse effects and optimize treatment efficacy.
693.technical_citations:
Bousman CA, Stevenson JM, Ramsey LB, et al. Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) Guideline for CYP2D6, CYP2C19, CYP2B6, SLC6A4, and HTR2A Genotypes and Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor Antidepressants. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2023 Jul;114(1):51-68.

 
 Chen G, Højer AM, Areberg J, et al. Vortioxetine: Clinical Pharmacokinetics and Drug Interactions. Clin Pharmacokinet. 2018 Jun;57(6):673-686.

 Zhang X, Cai Y, Hu X, et al. Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Vortioxetine for the Treatment of Major Depressive Disorder in Adults. Front Psychiatry. 2022 Jun 24;13:922648.

694.name: Allopurinol (Dosage)
694.description: Allopurinol is the medicine used in cases where too much uric acid is produced, which can lead to excessive levels in the blood or hyperuricemia. This may include gout or kidney stones (renal calculi), certain types of kidney diseases or cancer patients.
694.overview:
Allopurinol works by inhibiting the enzyme xanthine oxidase, which is responsible for converting purines into uric acid. Allopurinol prevents gout attacks and the formation of urate crystals in tissues and joints.

The dose of allopurinol varies depending on the severity of the condition, kidney function, and the patient's response to treatment. Generally, treatment begins with low doses (100 mg/day for people with normal kidney function) that are gradually increased until adequate control of uric acid levels is achieved.

SIDE EFFECTS

Skin rashes
Gastrointestinal discomfort (nausea, diarrhea)
Dizziness or drowsiness

PRECAUTIONS

Exceptionally, allopurinol may cause severe allergic reactions such as hypersensitivity syndrome, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis. Severe reactions include fever, skin rash, blisters and peeling of the skin, joint pain, and abnormalities in blood and liver function tests. Patients with the HLA-B*5801 allele, not evaluated here, have a higher probability of suffering adverse reactions. This allele is more common in Asian populations, although it may also be present in Caucasians.

PHARMACOLOGICAL INTERACTIONS

Allopurinol can interact with other medications, enhancing the toxicity of certain immunosuppressants (azathioprine, cyclosporine, and mercaptopurine) and increasing the effect of coumarin anticoagulants and the bronchodilator theophylline. Allopurinol may increase the risk of skin reactions if administered together with certain antibiotics such as ampicillin/amoxicillin. Allopurinol elimination increases with concomitant administration of probenecid, benzbromarone, sulfinpyrazone, or high doses of salicylates.

BRAND NAMES

Aloprim®
Urosin®
Zyloric®
Zyloprim®
694.dose:
The Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group (DPWG) guideline includes specific therapeutic guidelines based on the ABCG2 gene variant c.421C>A or p.Gln141Lys (rs2231142) for allopurinol dosing.

The efficacy of allopurinol is reduced in people carrying the c.421C>A variant. This decreases renal and intestinal uric acid excretion, meaning that stronger inhibition of uric acid production by allopurinol is required to achieve the desired blood uric acid concentration.

The DPWG guideline recommends using 1.25 times the standard dose in patients carrying one copy of the c.421C>A variant (TC genotype) and 1.4 times the standard dose in patients with two copies of c.421C>A (TT genotype).
694.technical_description:
The gene *ABCG2 (ATP Binding Cassette Subfamily G Member 2)* encodes a transporter protein involved in the renal and intestinal excretion of uric acid (urate). In addition, studies suggest that variants in *ABCG2* may influence the renal and intestinal excretion of allopurinol and its active metabolite, oxipurinol. Therefore, it is suggested that allopurinol and oxipurinol could be substrates of the transporter (Wen et al., 2015).

Certain common genetic variants in the *ABCG2* gene can affect the function of the ABCG2 transporter. The most prominent variant is c.421C>A, p.Gln141Lys or (rs2231142), which reduces the function of the transporter protein and results in a lower amount of ABCG2 transporter on the cell surface. This variant has been associated with higher levels of uric acid in the blood and risk of gout, but the underlying mechanisms are not well understood.

The mechanism by which ABCG2 alters the response to allopurinol remains unclear. It is suggested that the ABCG2 c.421C>A variant could increase the concentrations of allopurinol and oxipurinol in renal tubular cells and decrease the concentration in the tubular fluid, thereby reducing the inhibition of uric acid transporter pumps in the kidney and, therefore, the response to allopurinol (Wallace et al., 2018).

The c.421C>A variant is relatively common in certain populations. It is more common in people of Asian descent, where it is found in approximately 25-35% of individuals. In European populations, the frequency is significantly lower, generally around 15%.
694.technical_citations:
Eckenstaler R, Benndorf RA. The Role of ABCG2 in the Pathogenesis of Primary Hyperuricemia and Gout-An Update. Int J Mol Sci. 2021 Jun 22;22(13):6678.

van der Pol KH, Nijenhuis M, Soree B, et al. Dutch pharmacogenetics working group guideline for the gene-drug interaction of ABCG2, HLA-B and Allopurinol, and MTHFR, folic acid and methotrexate. Eur J Hum Genet. 2024 Feb;32(2):155-162.

Wallace MC, Roberts RL, Nanavati P, et al. Association between ABCG2 rs2231142 and poor response to allopurinol: replication and meta-analysis. Rheumatology (Oxford). 2018 Apr 1;57(4):656-660.

Wen CC, Yee SW, Liang X, et al. Genome-wide association study identifies ABCG2 (BCRP) as an allopurinol transporter and a determinant of drug response. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2015 May;97(5):518-25.

Woodward OM, Köttgen A, Coresh J, et al. Identification of a urate transporter, ABCG2, with a common functional polymorphism causing gout. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2009 Jun 23;106(25):10338-42.

 White WB, Saag KG, Becker MA, et al.Cardiovascular Safety of Febuxostat or Allopurinol in Patients with Gout. N Engl J Med. 2018 Mar 29;378(13):1200-1210.

696.name: Flucytosine (Adverse Reactions)
696.description: Flucytosine or 5-fluorocytosine is an antifungal medication used with other drugs such as amphotericin B to treat systemic infections.
696.overview:
Flucytosine is a systemic antifungal developed to treat severe infections caused by Candida and Cryptococcus.

The pharmacological effect of flucytosine only occurs in fungal cells and does not act on human cells. However, it is recommended that it be used in conjunction with other drugs to prevent drug resistance from occurring. It is usually combined with amphotericin B, which enhances its action.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Flucytosine is taken up by fungal cells and transformed into 5-fluorouracil, which is transformed into other metabolites inhibiting RNA and DNA synthesis of the microorganism.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Hypersensitivity to flucytosine or to any of the excipients. The use of flucytosine requires close monitoring and extreme caution in patients with hepatic, renal and hematological insufficiency.

Flucytosine is contraindicated during the first trimester of pregnancy.

Flucytosine should not be used in patients with known complete dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency due to the risk of severe toxicity.

SIDE EFFECTS

Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, cytopenias.

TRADE NAMES

Ancobon®
696.technical_description:
The dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) enzyme, encoded by the *DPYD* gene, is necessary to transform the drug 5-fluorouracil into dihydro-5-fluorouracil, a metabolite with lower toxicity than 5-fluorouracil. It is subsequently transformed into 5-fluoro-ureidopropionic acid and eliminated in the urine. DPD enzyme deficiency is a metabolic disorder that follows an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. If 5-fluorouracil is administered to carriers of *DPYD* variants associated with deficiency, an accumulation of toxic metabolites and an increased risk of adverse effects may occur compared to people with normal DPD metabolism.

The prodrugs capecitabine, tegafur, and flucytosine are transformed into 5-fluorouracil in our body and their metabolism also depends on DPD, therefore, patients with DPD deficiency also have a higher risk of suffering severe adverse reactions if they are treated with these prodrugs.

In 2020, the European Medicines Agency (EMA) indicated that it is advisable to analyze DPD before starting treatment with 5-fluorouracil, capecitabine, and tegafur. As flucytosine is used in serious infections that require immediate treatment, it is not always possible to perform genetic and/or enzymatic tests for DPD before starting treatment.

Treatment with drugs containing 5-fluorouracil, capecitabine, or tegafur is contraindicated in patients with complete DPD deficiency; flucytosine is also contraindicated in patients without DPD activity. In the case of partial or reduced DPD activity, the DPWG (Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group Guideline) recommends being alert to adverse effects (leukopenia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, and diarrhea), which usually appear 2-3 weeks after starting treatment, but there are no indications regarding its dosage since most patients with partial DPD deficiency tolerate flucytosine well.
696.technical_citations:
Donadio MDS, Carraro DM, Torrezan GT, de Mello CAL. Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) polymorphisms knocking on the door. Ecancermedicalscience. 2022 Jan 17;16:1344.
 Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group Guidelines. August 2020. 
Offer SM, Fossum CC, Wegner NJ, et al. Comparative functional analysis of DPYD variants of potential clinical relevance to dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity. Cancer Res. 2014 May 1;74(9):2545-54.
Padda IS, Parmar M. Flucytosine. 2024 Feb 28. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–.
Vermes A, Guchelaar HJ, Dankert J. Flucytosine: a review of its pharmacology, clinical indications, pharmacokinetics, toxicity and drug interactions. J Antimicrob Chemother. 2000 Aug;46(2):171-9.

697.name: Flurbiprofen (Dosage)
697.description: Flurbiprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used to relieve pain, reduce inflammation, and fever.
697.overview:
Flurbiprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) for the short-term relief of symptoms such as sore throat, dental, menstrual, muscle, and postoperative pain. It is used as a symptomatic treatment for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, soft tissue injuries such as bursitis and tendinitis, and after eye surgery.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Flurbiprofen works by inhibiting the activity of cyclooxygenase enzymes (COX-1 and COX-2), which are responsible for the production of prostaglandins, substances that cause inflammation, pain, and fever.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Allergy to NSAIDs, aspirin, or any of the components of the medicine.
History of gastrointestinal ulcers or gastrointestinal bleeding.
Flurbiprofen should not be used with high doses of acetylsalicylic acid (more than 75 mg of Aspirin per day) or another NSAID medicine (such as celecoxib, ibuprofen, sodium diclofenac).
Severe kidney or liver problems.
Heart problems or high cardiovascular risk.
Last trimester of pregnancy.

SIDE EFFECTS

The most frequent side effects are headache, dizziness, irritation and sore throat, itching and pruritus on the skin, mouth ulcers, and gastrointestinal discomfort. Allergic reactions such as asthma, skin rashes, difficulty breathing, palpitations, etc. may exceptionally occur. In case of an allergic reaction, stop taking the medicine and consult your doctor immediately.

Since flurbiprofen may increase the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and cardiovascular events, it is recommended to use the lowest effective dose for the shortest possible time and under medical supervision, especially in patients at higher risk.

TRADE NAMES

Ansaid®
Strefen®
Froben®
Ocufen®
697.dose: The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) dosing guideline for celecoxib, flurbiprofen, ibuprofen, and lornoxicam recommends initiating treatment with 25-50% of the lowest recommended starting dose for CYP2C9 poor metabolizers, and initiating treatment with the lowest recommended starting dose for CYP2C9 intermediate metabolizers who have two reduced function alleles or one normal and one loss-of-function allele. In CYP2C9 intermediate metabolizers who have one functional allele along with one reduced function allele, it is recommended to initiate treatment with the minimum effective dose as in normal metabolizers.
697.technical_description:
Cytochrome CYP2C9 is the main hepatic enzyme involved in the metabolism of flurbiprofen. At least 71% of flurbiprofen is metabolized to 4'-hydroxyflurbiprofen (inactive form) via CYP2C9. Both flurbiprofen and 4'-hydroxyflurbiprofen are eliminated as acylglucuronides through reactions mediated by UGT2B7 and UGT1A9. Individuals with decreased CYP2C9 activity, such as CYP2C9 intermediate and poor metabolizers, have increased exposure to flurbiprofen, which may increase the risk of adverse effects.

The possibility of drug interactions should be considered when initiating NSAID treatment. Carriers of a reduced function or loss-of-function CYP2C9 allele have an increased risk of bleeding that is further increased with concomitant use of warfarin or other coumarin anticoagulants with NSAIDs, compared to normal metabolizers. Therefore, the CPIC guideline recommends avoiding this combination of drugs in CYP2C9 intermediate and poor metabolizers.
697.technical_citations:
Dean L. Flurbiprofen Therapy and CYP2C9 Genotype. 2019 Feb 11. In: Pratt VM, Scott SA, Pirmohamed M, et al., editors. Medical Genetics Summaries [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Center for Biotechnology Information (US); 2012-.
Loisios-Konstantinidis I, Cristofoletti R, Jamei M, et al. Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Modeling to Predict the Impact of CYP2C9 Genetic Polymorphisms, Co-Medication and Formulation on the Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics of Flurbiprofen. Pharmaceutics. 2020 Nov 2;12(11):1049.
Theken KN, Lee CR, Gong L, et al.  Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline (CPIC) for CYP2C9 and Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2020 Aug;108(2):191-200.

698.name: Hydrocodone (Dosage)
698.description: Hydrocodone is an opioid medication that is primarily used for the relief of moderate to severe pain, and in some cases, for the treatment of cough in patients for whom other medications are not effective.
698.dose: The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) dosing guideline for hydrocodone recommends initiating therapy using the age- or weight-specific dose recommended on the label regardless of CYP2D6 genotype. However, in patients with CYP2D6 intermediate or poor metabolizer genotypes, the guideline suggests monitoring closely for analgesic response. If this is insufficient, use of an alternative medication should be considered.
698.technical_description:
The cytochrome CYP2D6 is the main hepatic enzyme involved in the metabolism of hydrocodone, it is responsible for transforming it into its active metabolite, hydromorphone. People with certain variants of this gene may metabolize hydrocodone more rapidly or slowly.

 CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizers (UM) might be expected to process hydrocodone rapidly, compared to normal metabolizers, which could increase effects and the risk of side effects. However, there is not enough evidence to recommend avoiding hydrocodone in CYP2D6 UM and the CPIC guideline does not recommend dose changes.

 In intermediate and poor CYP2D6 metabolizers, studies indicate a reduction in the formation of the active metabolite hydromorphone, but more evidence is needed to clarify if there is a significant decrease in analgesia. For this reason, in these cases, the use of the dose recommended on the hydrocodone label is also advised. However, if there is no response to hydrocodone in intermediate or poor CYP2D6 metabolizers, the use of an alternative analgesic (non-opioid or an opioid not affected by CYP2D6 phenotype) should be considered.
698.technical_citations:
Crews KR, Monte AA, Huddart R, et al. Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline for CYP2D6, OPRM1, and COMT Genotypes and Select Opioid Therapy. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2021 Oct;110(4):888-896.
Peiró AM. Pharmacogenetics in Pain Treatment. Adv Pharmacol. 2018;83:247-273.
Smith DM, Stevenson JM, Ho TT, et al. Pharmacogenetics: A Precision Medicine Approach to Combatting the Opioid Epidemic. J Am Coll Clin Pharm. 2022 Feb;5(2):239-250.

699.name: Lornoxicam (Dosage)
699.description: Lornoxicam is a medication belonging to the class of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). It is mainly used for the relief of pain and inflammation.
699.dose:
The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) dosing guideline for lornoxicam suggests the following adjustments based on CYP2C9 metabolizer phenotype:

Intermediate metabolizers with one functional allele and one reduced function allele are recommended to start treatment with the recommended dose.
Intermediate metabolizers with one loss-of-function allele or two reduced function alleles: it is advised to use the lowest recommended dose as the initial dose and increase the dose until the clinical effect or the maximum recommended dose is reached with caution, carefully monitoring blood pressure and kidney function during treatment.
CYP2C9 poor metabolizers: start treatment with 25-50% of the lowest recommended dose. The dose should not be increased until steady state is reached (8 days). Carefully monitor adverse effects blood pressure and kidney function. Alternatively, consider an alternative drug that is not significantly affected by variants in CYP2C9.
699.technical_description:
Cytochrome CYP2C9 is the main hepatic enzyme involved in lornoxicam metabolism. This cytochrome is responsible for transforming it into its inactive metabolite, 5'-hydroxylornoxicam, so that it is eliminated from the body in 3-5 hours. Only one third of lornoxicam is excreted in its active form through urine.

People who carry at least one copy of a CYP2C9 loss-of-function allele or two copies of a CYP2C9 reduced-function allele have a reduction in lornoxicam metabolism and greater exposure to the drug, which could increase the likelihood of adverse effects. In the case of intermediate metabolizers who have one functional copy of CYP2C9 and one copy of a reduced-function allele, such as CYP2C9*2, there is a slight reduction in lornoxicam metabolism, but it is not significant, and the CPIC guideline recommends starting treatment with the standard dose. However, when assessing the most appropriate dose for the patient, other factors that may influence drug elimination and increase the risk of adverse effects, such as advanced age and liver function, should be taken into consideration.
699.technical_citations:
Choi CI, Kim MJ, Jang CG, et al. Effects of the CYP2C9*1/*13 genotype on the pharmacokinetics of lornoxicam. Basic Clin Pharmacol Toxicol. 2011 Dec;109(6):476-80.
Theken KN, Lee CR, Gong L, et al. Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline (CPIC) for CYP2C9 and Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2020 Aug;108(2):191-200.

700.name: Meloxicam (Dosage)
700.description: Meloxicam is an oxicam-class medication that belongs to the nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) used for pain management. Like other NSAIDs, it is primarily metabolized by CYP2C9.
700.overview:
Meloxicam is an NSAID analgesic used to treat osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis in adults. Meloxicam is approved for use in some countries, such as the United States, for the management of pain and inflammation associated with juvenile idiopathic arthritis.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Meloxicam is a potent inhibitor of cyclooxygenase enzymes (COX-1 and COX-2), which works by reducing the synthesis of prostaglandins, substances that mediate pain, inflammation, and fever. It acts preferentially on COX-2, which reduces the risk and severity of gastrointestinal adverse effects typical of NSAIDs, although not completely.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Treatment with meloxicam is contraindicated in the following cases:

Patients with hypersensitivity to NSAIDs, including acetylsalicylic acid.
In some countries, such as Spain, it is contraindicated in children and adolescents.
History of gastrointestinal perforation or bleeding related to NSAID use.
Presence of active or previous gastrointestinal ulcers or bleeding.
Cerebrovascular hemorrhages or other types of active bleeding.
During the third trimester of pregnancy.
Patients with severe kidney, liver, and heart problems.

SIDE EFFECTS

The most frequent adverse reactions are indigestion, nausea, abdominal pain, vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, gas, and headache.

INTERACTIONS

Meloxicam can interact with multiple medications, so it is recommended to inform your doctor of all the medications you are taking. Meloxicam interacts with NSAIDs (such as acetylsalicylic acid and ibuprofen) and anticoagulant agents (such as warfarin) and antiplatelet agents (e.g., clopidogrel) and can lead to an increased risk of bleeding. It also interacts with corticosteroids, immunosuppressants (cyclosporine and tacrolimus), lithium, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), diuretics (spironolactone), oral antidiabetics (sulfonylureas and nateglinide), methotrexate, deferasirox, some antibiotics (trimethoprim), among other medications.

BRAND NAMES

Anjeso®
Qmiiz®
Mobic®
Vivlodex®
Zynrelef®
700.dose:
The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) dosing guideline for meloxicam suggests the following adjustments based on the CYP2C9 metabolizer profile:

Intermediate metabolizers with one functional allele and one reduced function allele are recommended to start treatment with the recommended dose.
Intermediate metabolizers with one loss-of-function allele or two reduced-function alleles: it is advised to use 50% of the lowest recommended dose as the starting dose and increase the dose to achieve the clinical effect or 50% of the maximum recommended dose with caution, carefully monitoring blood pressure and renal function during treatment.
CYP2C9 poor metabolizers: select an alternative non-drug that is not significantly affected by variants in CYP2C9 or choose an NSAID with a shorter half-life.
700.technical_description:
Cytochrome CYP2C9 is the main hepatic enzyme involved in meloxicam metabolism. Cytochrome CYP3A4 is also involved in its elimination, but to a lesser extent. Both transform meloxicam into 5'-hydroxymethylmeloxicam, which is subsequently transformed into 5'-carboxymeloxicam and excreted in feces and urine.

Meloxicam has a plasma half-life of 20 hours, which is longer than other anti-inflammatory drugs such as celecoxib and ibuprofen. A reduction in CYP2C9 activity increases the risk of adverse effects, especially in NSAIDs with a longer plasma half-life.

In the case of intermediate metabolizers who have one functional copy of CYP2C9 and one copy of a reduced function allele, such as CYP2C9*2, there is a slight reduction in meloxicam metabolism, and the CPIC guideline recommends starting treatment with the standard dose and being alert for adverse effects, especially in patients with other risk factors, such as advanced age and liver problems.

In the case of patients with a more reduced intermediate CYP2C9 metabolism, who have one loss-of-function allele or two reduced-function alleles, the CPIC guideline recommends starting treatment with 50% of the lowest recommended dose and, at least 7 days after starting treatment, gradually increasing the dose with caution, without exceeding 50% of the maximum recommended dose. In the case of CYP2C9 poor metabolizers, CPIC advises using an alternative analgesic not primarily metabolized by CYP2C9 or using an NSAID with a shorter half-life than meloxicam.
700.technical_citations: Theken KN, Lee CR, Gong L, et al. Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline (CPIC) for CYP2C9 and Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2020 Aug;108(2):191-200.

701.name: Piroxicam (Dosage)
701.description: Piroxicam is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used in the treatment of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and ankylosing spondylitis to relieve pain and inflammation. Piroxicam is primarily metabolized by CYP2C9.
701.overview:
Piroxicam is primarily metabolized by CYP2C9. People with reduced CYP2C9 activity have a higher exposure to piroxicam and a higher risk of side effects.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Piroxicam is an inhibitor of cyclooxygenase enzymes that acts by reducing the synthesis of prostaglandins, substances that mediate pain, inflammation and fever. Piroxicam also inhibits the migration of leukocytes to the inflammatory focus and inhibits platelet aggregation.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Treatment with piroxicam is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to piroxicam or to any of the components of the medicine, if they have had reactions to acetylsalicylic acid or another NSAID, a history of asthma, urticaria or other allergic-type reactions after taking acetylsalicylic acid or another NSAID, and after coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery. In patients with gastric ulcers, a history of gastrointestinal bleeding or perforation, patients with inflammatory bowel disease, patients with severe heart or liver failure, and in patients who are taking anticoagulants. Piroxicam should also be avoided in pregnant women from the third trimester onwards and during lactation.

SIDE EFFECTS

The most frequent side effects are mild headache, dizziness, drowsiness, nausea, abdominal pain, dyspepsia, diarrhea, vomiting, alterations in blood cell count, loss of appetite, increased blood sugar levels, and swelling of the ankles.

Like all NSAIDs, piroxicam increases the risk of serious cardiovascular problems (myocardial infarction and stroke), as well as severe gastrointestinal disorders such as bleeding, ulceration and perforation.

INTERACTIONS

Piroxicam may interact with corticosteroids, anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, lithium, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor antidepressants (SSRIs), immunosuppressants (cyclosporine and tacrolimus), sulfonylureas, methotrexate at doses equal to or greater than 15mg/week (at low doses if there is combined treatment, blood values ​​and kidney function should be closely monitored).

BRAND NAMES

Feldene®
701.dose:
The standard dose of piroxicam for osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis in adults is 20 mg once daily. However, in all patients, the lowest effective dose of piroxicam should be used for the shortest possible time, consistent with individual patient treatment goals.

The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) dosing guideline for piroxicam suggests the following adjustments based on the CYP2C9 metabolizer profile:

Intermediate metabolizers who have one functional allele and one reduced function allele are recommended to start treatment with the recommended dose. Carefully monitor for adverse effects, blood pressure, and liver function, especially in patients with other risk factors such as advanced age and hepatic impairment.
Intermediate metabolizers who have one loss-of-function allele or two reduced function alleles and CYP2C9 poor metabolizers: it is advisable to use an alternative treatment that is not significantly affected by variants in CYP2C9.
701.technical_description:
Piroxicam was the first oxicam-type drug to be introduced in the United States in 1982; other oxicam drugs include isoxicam, meloxicam, tenoxicam, and lornoxicam.

Plasma concentrations of piroxicam generally reach peak levels 3-5 hours after administration and take 50 hours to be completely eliminated. The main cytochrome involved in its transformation to 5'-hydroxypiroxicam and hepatic elimination is CYP2C9. Subsequently, it is glucuronidated and excreted in urine and feces, in a ratio of twice as much in urine as in feces. Only 5% of piroxicam is excreted directly without being modified. It has been suggested in multiple studies that cytochrome CYP2C8 could also be involved in the metabolism of piroxicam and other NSAIDs, but the studies are inconsistent.

Variants affecting CYP2C9 function (reduced function and loss-of-function alleles) have an influence on piroxicam metabolism and drug exposure. If there is a reduction in CYP2C9 activity, higher plasma levels of piroxicam are reached and there is a greater risk of adverse effects. In addition, since piroxicam has a long half-life, the risk of adverse effects is amplified compared to other NSAIDs with a shorter half-life.

In CYP2C9 poor metabolizers and intermediate metabolizers (with one loss-of-function allele or two reduced-function alleles), the CPIC guideline recommends that an alternative drug not primarily metabolized by CYP2C9 be chosen, and that if an NSAID metabolized by CYP2C9 is used, it should have a shorter half-life. In intermediate metabolizers with one functional allele and one reduced-function allele, CYP2C9 activity is slightly reduced, and this does not cause a significant increase in the drug in plasma; however, they have a slightly increased risk of adverse effects compared to normal metabolizers. Therefore, it is recommended to assess other risk factors that the patient may present and influence the elimination of piroxicam, such as age and liver function.
701.technical_citations:
Dean L. Piroxicam Therapy and CYP2C9 Genotype. 2019 Feb 11. In: Pratt VM, Scott SA, Pirmohamed M, Esquivel B, Kattman BL, Malheiro AJ, editors. Medical Genetics Summaries [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Center for Biotechnology Information (US); 2012–.
Perini JA, Vianna-Jorge R, Brogliato AR, Suarez-Kurtz G. Influence of CYP2C9 genotypes on the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of piroxicam. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2005 Oct;78(4):362-9.
Theken KN, Lee CR, Gong L, et al. Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline (CPIC) for CYP2C9 and Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2020 Aug;108(2):191-200.

703.name: Siponimod (Dosage)
703.description: Siponimod is a drug from the sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator family, used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. It was approved in March 2019 for the treatment of patients with relapsing forms of the pathology. Its use requires prior genetic analysis in some cases, due to hepatic metabolism dependent on CYP2C9.
703.overview:
Siponimod is an immunomodulatory drug used to treat active secondary progressive multiple sclerosis (SPMS). It acts as a selective modulator of sphingosine-1-phosphate receptors (S1P1 and S1P5), which helps reduce the migration of lymphocytes to the central nervous system and decrease inflammation and neuronal damage.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Siponimod is a selective modulator of sphingosine 1-phosphate receptors on lymphocytes. By acting as a functional antagonist of these receptors, the drug prevents lymphocytes from exiting the lymph nodes, reducing the recirculation of these cells in the central nervous system and limiting central inflammation.

Siponimod is indicated for the treatment of adults with secondary progressive multiple sclerosis with active disease, with relapses or intense inflammatory activity.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Treatment with siponimod is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug or any of its components, immunodeficiency syndrome, history of meningitis or leukoencephalopathy, cancer, liver failure, recent stroke, and during pregnancy.

SIDE EFFECTS

The most frequently reported, although infrequent, side effects are dizziness, pain in the arms, legs, hands or feet, diarrhea, and headache. Some of the side effects can be serious, such as slow heart rate, vomiting, fever, stiff neck, sudden and severe headache, difficulty breathing, vomiting, or swelling in the hands, among others.

Siponimod may also increase the risk of developing skin cancer, so patients taking it should examine their skin before and during treatment to detect changes.

INTERACTIONS

Although the use of siponimod in combination with other neoplastic or immunomodulatory treatments has not been studied, caution should be exercised during concomitant administration due to the risk of additive effects.

The use of siponimod is contraindicated with some antiarrhythmic treatments, calcium channel antagonists, or other compounds that decrease heart rate. Caution should also be taken with taking beta-blocker medications.

During treatment with siponimod and for 4 weeks after its discontinuation, the administration of live attenuated microorganism vaccines should be avoided.

TRADE NAMES

Mayzent®
703.dose:
Treatment should be initiated progressively over 5 days until the therapeutic dose is established. The standard starting dose is 0.25 mg once daily for the first two days, followed by 0.5 mg, 0.75 mg, 1.25 mg, and 2 mg on days 3, 4, 5, and 6, respectively.

According to recommendations from the DPWG clinical guideline, individuals with an intermediate metabolizer (IM) profile for CYP2C9 have an increased risk of adverse effects, so the dose in them should be reduced by 50%. In the case of presenting a profile

The Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group (DPWG) dosing guideline for siponimod suggests the following adjustments based on the CYP2C9 metabolization profile:

Intermediate metabolizers (IM) with one functional allele and one reduced function allele: it is recommended to start treatment with the recommended dose. Carefully monitor for adverse effects.
Intermediate metabolizers (IM) with one loss-of-function allele or two reduced-function alleles: the use of 50% of the normal maintenance dose is recommended. Carefully monitor for adverse effects and exercise caution if the patient is also using a moderate CYP3A4 inducer.
Poor metabolizers (PM) CYP2C9: the risk of adverse effects increases considerably due to the increase in drug concentrations. It is advisable to use an alternative treatment that is not significantly influenced by variants in CYP2C9.
703.technical_description:
Siponimod belongs to the family of selective sphingosine 1-phosphate receptor modulators. This receptor belongs to the family of G protein-coupled receptors and is involved in various physiological processes, especially in the immune and nervous systems. These receptors have the ability to alter lymphocyte trafficking, with potential benefits for autoimmune diseases.

 Drugs of this nature, such as siponimod or fingolimod, are phosphorylated in the body and, once active, bind to receptors in the lymph nodes, preventing certain cells of the lymphoid lineage from entering the bloodstream and reaching the nervous system. In this way, in pathologies like multiple sclerosis, an autoimmune disease in which immune cells attack the myelin sheath, this myelin sheath remains intact for longer.

 Siponimod is metabolized, mainly by CYP2C9 and, to a lesser extent, by CYP3A4. Decreased metabolic activity of CYP2C9 is associated with higher blood concentrations of siponimod and an increased risk of side effects. Therefore, individuals with a poor metabolic profile, such as carriers of two copies of the CYP2C9*3 alleles, are contraindicated from taking siponimod. Carriers of one copy of a loss-of-function allele or two copies of a reduced-function allele are advised to take half of the standard maintenance dose.

 It should be noted that the dosage recommendations of some organizations, such as the FDA, only include alleles *1, *2, and *3 due to the lack of clinical data on other alleles. However, the DPWG recommends dose adjustments also taking into account the rest of the alleles.
703.technical_citations:
Tran QT, Baek IH, Han NY, Yun HY, Chae JW. The Effect of CYP2D6 Phenotypes on the Pharmacokinetics of Propafenone: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Pharmaceutics. 2022 Jul 11;14(7):1446.
Sunthankar SD, Kannankeril PJ, Gaedigk A, Radbill AE, Fish FA, Van Driest SL. Influence of CYP2D6 genetic variation on adverse events with propafenone in the pediatric and young adult population. Clin Transl Sci. 2022 Jul;15(7):1787-1795.

704.name: Tenoxicam (Dosage)
704.description: Tenoxicam is a medication from the group of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) used to relieve pain and inflammation in disorders such as rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and other musculoskeletal diseases. It is primarily metabolized by CYP2C9, so people with reduced activity for this cytochrome may have greater exposure to the drug.
704.overview:
Tenoxicam is a drug belonging to the family of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) used in the treatment of pain, especially in conditions such as rheumatic diseases, as well as lumbago, gout, myositis, bursitis, or sprains. It acts by inhibiting the COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes, which reduces the production of prostaglandins, substances responsible for pain and inflammation.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Tenoxicam is an inhibitor of cyclooxygenase enzymes, COX-1 and COX-2, which acts by reducing the synthesis of prostaglandins, substances that mediate pain, inflammation, and fever. Tenoxicam also inhibits the migration of leukocytes to the inflammatory focus and inhibits platelet aggregation.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Treatment with tenoxicam is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug or any of its components, a history of asthma, urticaria, or other allergic reactions after taking acetylsalicylic acid or other NSAIDs.

It should also be avoided in patients with a history of gastrointestinal bleeding or ulcers or severe heart failure. It should be avoided in pregnant women and during breastfeeding.

SIDE EFFECTS

The most frequently reported side effects, although infrequent, are mild headache, dizziness, fatigue, sleep disturbances, loss of appetite, dry mouth, itching, rash, urticaria, increased blood urea, increased liver activity, palpitations, or high blood pressure.

Like all NSAIDs, tenoxicam increases the risk of serious cardiovascular problems (myocardial infarction and stroke), as well as severe gastrointestinal disturbances, such as bleeding, ulceration, and perforation.

INTERACTIONS

The administration of other pain medications, such as acetylsalicylic acid or NSAIDs, can increase the frequency of gastrointestinal side effects. Caution should be exercised when taking it with methotrexate, as serious adverse effects such as blood problems, diarrhea, inflammation, or ulcers may occur.

Combination with lithium can cause undesirable effects such as tremor, vomiting, skin redness, and fluid retention.

In any case, a doctor should be consulted when taking medications containing the following active ingredients together with tenoxicam: NSAIDs, salicylates, diuretics, alpha-adrenergic blockers, hydrochlorothiazide, anticoagulants, antidiabetics, or corticosteroids, among others.

TRADE NAMES

Reutenox®Mobiflex®Novo-tenoxicamTilcotilApo-Tenoxicam
704.dose:
The standard dose of tenoxicam in adults is 20 mg once daily. However, the lowest effective dose should be used in all patients for the shortest possible time, consistent with the treatment goals of each individual.

The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) dosing guideline for piroxicam suggests the following adjustments based on CYP2C9 metabolizer status:

Intermediate metabolizers who have one functional allele and one reduced function allele, it is recommended to initiate treatment with the recommended dose. Carefully monitor for adverse effects, blood pressure, and liver function, especially in patients with other risk factors such as advanced age and liver failure.
Intermediate metabolizers who have one loss-of-function allele or two reduced-function alleles, and CYP2C9 poor metabolizers: it is advised to use an alternative therapy that is not significantly influenced by variants in CYP2C9.
704.technical_description:
Tenoxicam belongs to the oxicam family along with piroxicam, isoxicam, meloxicam, and lornoxicam. Its use was approved in 1987.

Administered orally, it is absorbed quickly and completely. The maximum plasma concentration is reached two hours after its intake. In blood, more than 99% of the drug circulates bound to albumin. The elimination half-life of tenoxicam is approximately 72 hours.

The main cytochrome involved in its transformation to inactive metabolites is CYP2C9. It is subsequently excreted mainly in the urine.

Variants that affect CYP2C9 function (reduction and loss-of-function alleles) have an influence on the metabolism of tenoxicam and exposure to the drug. If there is a reduction in CYP2C9 activity, higher plasma levels of piroxicam are reached and there is a greater risk of adverse effects. Furthermore, since tenoxicam has a long half-life, the risk of adverse effects is amplified compared to other NSAIDs with a shorter half-life.

In CYP2C9 poor metabolizers and intermediate metabolizers (with one loss-of-function allele or two reduced-function alleles), the CPIC guideline recommends that an alternative drug not primarily metabolized by CYP2C9 should be chosen and that, if an NSAID metabolized by CYP2C9 is used, it should have a shorter half-life. In intermediate metabolizers with one functional allele and one reduced-function allele, there is no noticeable increase in the amount of the drug in plasma, although these individuals have a slightly increased risk of adverse effects compared to normal metabolizers.
704.technical_citations:
Díaz-Villamarín X, Piñar-Morales R, et al. Pharmacogenetics of siponimod: A systematic review. Biomed Pharmacother. 2022 Sep;153:113536.
 Kane M. Siponimod Therapy and CYP2C9 Genotype. 2023 Aug 9. In: Pratt VM, Scott SA, Pirmohamed M, et al., editors. Medical Genetics Summaries [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Center for Biotechnology Information (US).

705.name: Brivaracetam (Dosage)
705.description: Brivaracetam is an anticonvulsant drug used in the treatment of epilepsy. It is metabolized by cytochrome CYP2C19, and defects in this can lead to the appearance of side effects if the dose is not well regulated.
705.overview:
Brivaracetam is an anticonvulsant medication used in the treatment of patients with epilepsy in adults and children aged 4 years and older. It is often used in conjunction with other medications to control seizures that only affect a part of the brain (so-called "partial-onset seizures").

 

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Brivaracetam works by reducing abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Brivaracetam binds to the SV2A protein, which is present in the synaptic vesicle and is thought to be responsible for some seizure processes. The binding of brivaracetam to SV2A appears to be the main mechanism of action of this drug.

 

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Hypersensitivity to brivaracetam and other pyrrolidone derivatives.

 

PRECAUTIONS

The safety and efficacy of brivaracetam have not yet been established in children under 4 years of age.

Taking brivaracetam may pose a risk of suicidal thoughts/behaviors, so it is very important to monitor the patient's psychology.

The prescription of brivaracetam is not recommended in kidney patients undergoing dialysis, as there is no safety or efficacy data.

In patients with liver insufficiency, extreme caution should be exercised before prescribing brivaracetam, as there is no safety or efficacy data for these patients. Adjust the dose very rigorously and monitor the patient if it is decided to prescribe this medication.

 

SIDE EFFECTS

The most common side effects are: constipation, nausea, vomiting, extreme tiredness, or lack of energy.

More serious but less common side effects include: swelling of the face, throat, tongue, lips, and eyes, difficulty swallowing or breathing, hoarseness, hallucinations, delusions, suicidal thoughts and behaviors.

 

TRADE NAME

Briviact®
705.dose: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) recommends that CYP2C19 intermediate metabolizers (IM) and CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (PM) may have increased systemic concentrations and increased risk of adverse effects with brivaracetam treatment compared to normal or extensive metabolizers (EM) CYP2C19. It recommends that brivaracetam dose reduction be considered in PM CYP2C19 patients and in patients using other drugs that inhibit CYP2C19.
705.technical_description:
Brivaracetam is mainly metabolized through hydrolysis by amidase enzymes, producing an inactive metabolite that is excreted. To a lesser extent, it is metabolized through CYP2C19-dependent hydroxylation.

Individuals with variants that markedly decrease CYP2C19 activity are expected to have increased exposure to brivaracetam at standard doses as they are less able to transform the drug to its inactive form for excretion.

Plasma brivaracetam levels are increased by approximately 22% and 42% in IM and PM CYP2C19 individuals, respectively, versus CYP2C19 MS. There are no recommendations for IM CYP2C19, but for PM CYP2C19 patients the FDA advises a dose decrease to minimize the risk of side effects; this dose decrease should also be considered in patients who are normal or EM CYP2C19 who are taking any CYP2C19 inhibitor drug.

In addition, drug interactions should be considered when prescribing brivaracetam. If brivaracetam is coadministered with rifampicin, the dose of brivaracetam should be increased (up to a maximum of twice the recommended dose) since rifampicin decreases plasma concentrations of brivaracetam as it is an inducer of CYP2C19. This increase in dosage is probably not necessary in CYP2C19 PM patients. When carbamazepine is prescribed together with brivaracetam, carbamazepine exposure increases, so a reduction in carbamazepine concentration should be considered in these cases. Brivaracetam can increase phenytoin plasma levels, so in these cases plasma phenytoin levels should be monitored in order to assess whether to continue or change phenytoin treatment.
705.technical_citations:
Dean L.  Brivaracetam Therapy and CYP2C19 Genotype. 2018 May 15. In: Pratt VM, Scott SA, Pirmohamed M, Esquivel B, Kattman BL, Malheiro AJ, editors. Medical Genetics Summaries [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Center for Biotechnology Information (US); 2012-.

U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Table of Pharmacogenetic Associations.

706.name: Clobazam (Adverse Reactions)
706.description: Clobazam is a benzodiazepine used as an anxiolytic and in some cases of epilepsy. Clobazam is mostly metabolized by the cytochrome CYP2C19 and polymorphic variants of this cytochrome may have an impact on treatment.
706.overview:
Clobazam is a long half-life benzodiazepine used as an anxiolytic and in combination with other drugs to treat seizures caused by epilepsy.

Its mechanism of action is to increase GABA activity by facilitating its binding to the GABAergic receptor.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Hypersensitivity to clobazam and benzodiazepines.

Myasthenia gravis, sleep apnea syndrome, severe respiratory insufficiency, severe hepatic insufficiency, angle-closure glaucoma.

Do not take during lactation.

PRECAUTIONS

Do not administer to children under 3 years of age, except in exceptional cases where treatment is essential, or in the elderly. Psychiatric and paradoxical reactions are more frequent in children and elderly.

Ingestion of clobazam may cause hepatic failure, renal failure and acute or chronic respiratory failure.

Individuals who are poor metabolizers of clobazam (see technical report) may require dose adjustment.

In patients with pre-existing muscle weakness or spinal or cerebellar ataxia, special monitoring and dose reduction of the drug should be carried out.

After continued use of the drug there is a risk of tolerance and physical-psychic dependence. Abrupt discontinuation after continuous use causes withdrawal syndrome. Do not use in anxiety associated with depression or as primary treatment of psychotic illness.

SIDE EFFECTS

Drowsiness, affective dullness, reduced alertness, confusion, fatigue, headache, dizziness, vertigo, muscular hypotonia, muscular weakness, ataxia, diplopia, amnesia, depression, psychiatric and paradoxical reactions, dry mouth, constipation, decreased appetite, nausea, weight gain and dependence.

BRAND NAMES

Noiafen ®Onfi ®
706.technical_description:
Clobazam is mostly metabolized by the cytochrome CYP2C19 and polymorphic variants of this cytochrome have an impact on the treatment of epilepsy.

Concentrations of the active metabolite of clobazam, N-desmethylclobazam, are 3 to 5 times higher in CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (PM) than in extensive or normal metabolizers (EM) and intermediate metabolizers (IM). Poor CYP2C19 metabolism leads to increased exposure to the active metabolite of clobazam and, therefore, increased risk of adverse reactions. For this reason, the FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration) recommends that in CYP2C19 PM the dose of ONFI® (clobazam) be reduced. The starting dose should be 5 mg/day and adjusted slowly and carefully taking into account the recommendations for PM CYP2C19 in the drug package insert.

The frequency of PM CYP2C19 varies among populations and is higher in Asians (18-23%). In Caucasians the frequency of PM is 2-5%.
706.technical_citations:
Tolbert D, Larsen F. A Comprehensive Overview of the Clinical Pharmacokinetics of Clobazam. J Clin Pharmacol. 2019 Jan;59(1):7-19.

Yamamoto Y, Takahashi Y, Imai K, et al. Influence of CYP2C19 polymorphism and concomitant antiepileptic drugs on serum clobazam and N-desmethyl clobazam concentrations in patients with epilepsy. Ther Drug Monit. 2013 Jun;35(3):305-12.

707.name: Diazepam (Adverse Reactions)
707.description: Diazepam is a drug of the benzodiazepine family with anxiolytic, myorelaxant, anticonvulsant and sedative properties. People with reduced CYP2C19 activity may be exposed to a higher dose of the drug.
707.overview:
Diazepam is a benzodiazepine derivative with anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, anticonvulsant, and sedative properties. It facilitates the binding of the neurotransmitter GABA to its neuronal receptor and increases its activity, thus producing a relaxation of central nervous system activity. It acts on the limbic system, thalamus, and hypothalamus. It does not produce peripheral autonomic nervous system blockade or extrapyramidal side effects.

Diazepam is effective in controlling anxiety, seizures, and, in some cases, bipolar disorder. However, it is essential to take precautions, as its prolonged use can lead to adverse effects or physical and psychological dependence.

Contraindications

Diazepam is contraindicated in individuals with hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines or with dependence on other substances, including alcohol (except for the treatment of acute withdrawal reactions). It is also contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis, sleep apnea syndrome, severe respiratory insufficiency, severe hepatic insufficiency, narrow-angle glaucoma (only with rectal, oral administration), severe chronic hypercapnia (only with oral administration).

Diazepam is contraindicated during pregnancy due to its teratogenic potential. Studies have shown that its use during pregnancy can negatively affect fetal development, increasing the risk of malformations and complications in pregnancy. Additionally, the use of diazepam during pregnancy can cause sedative effects in the newborn, which can lead to respiratory difficulties and problems with thermal regulation.

Diazepam is contraindicated in patients with severe traumatic brain injuries due to the risk of respiratory depression and central nervous system side effects, which could worsen the patient's condition.
