Diazepam is contraindicated in patients with ocular diseases such as narrow-angle glaucoma, as it can increase intraocular pressure and worsen the condition.

Diazepam is also contraindicated in neonates due to the risks associated with respiratory depression and sedative effects that can severely affect respiratory and cardiac function in newborns. Furthermore, neonates have a higher susceptibility to side effects due to their still immature nervous system.

Precautions

Precautions should be taken when administering diazepam to the elderly, to patients with renal insufficiency, mild or moderate hepatic insufficiency. Caution should be exercised when prescribing diazepam to children. Care should be taken when prescribing diazepam to patients with chronic respiratory insufficiency, porphyria, epilepsy, or with alcohol/drug dependence or a history of dependence.

It is essential to consider the risk of side effects such as anterograde amnesia, psychiatric or paradoxical reactions, and the possibility of developing physical and psychological dependence after prolonged use of the drug.

In patients with CNS lesions and epileptic seizures, extreme caution should be exercised as it may decrease cerebral circulation and blood oxygenation, potentially leading to irreversible brain damage. Risk of anterograde amnesia, psychiatric and paradoxical reactions.

After continuous use of diazepam, there is a risk of tolerance and dependence (physical and psychological). Abrupt discontinuation of treatment can trigger a withdrawal syndrome, which includes symptoms such as anxiety, insomnia, and in some cases, seizures.

Do not use in anxiety associated with depression or as primary treatment for psychotic illness.

Intravenous administration of diazepam carries a risk of apnea and/or cardiac arrest in the elderly, in very debilitated patients, or those with limited cardiac or pulmonary reserve.

Side Effects

Diazepam may cause drowsiness, emotional blunting, reduced alertness, confusion, fatigue, headache, dizziness, muscle weakness, ataxia or diplopia, amnesia, depression, psychiatric and paradoxical reactions; respiratory depression. The most common side effect of diazepam is drowsiness, which can interfere with daily activities. In addition, some people may experience dizziness or confusion, especially when starting treatment or increasing the dose.

Drug Interactions

The depressant effect of diazepam on the central nervous system (CNS) is potentiated by: neuroleptics, hypnotics, anxiolytics/sedatives, antidepressants, narcotic analgesics, antiepileptics, anesthetics, sedative antihistamines.

The sedative effect of diazepam is increased by: alcohol, cisapride, cimetidine, propofol, ethanol.

The action of diazepam is increased by: cytochrome P450 inhibitors, azole antifungals, isoniazid.

There is an additive depressant effect on the CNS and respiratory system with: barbiturates and centrally acting muscle relaxants.

Diazepam toxicity is increased by: ethinylestradiol and mestranol, fluoxetine, omeprazole, ketoconazole, fluvoxamine, valproic acid.

The effect of diazepam is decreased by: phenobarbital, carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampicin.

Diazepam increases the action of digoxin and alters the action of phenytoin.

BRAND NAMES

Stesolid ®
Valium ®
Aneurol®
Ansium®
Gobanal®  
Pacium® 
Tepazepan®
707.technical_description:
The clinical response to benzodiazepines, such as diazepam, shows a wide variability between individuals, largely influenced by genetic factors.

In the liver, diazepam is converted to its active metabolite nordiazepam by CYP2C19 and CYP3A4, and to temazepam via CYP3A4. Both are subsequently oxidized to produce oxacepam and temazepam, both of which are minor active metabolites. Oxacepam is conjugated with glucuronide and excreted in the urine.

Although both cytochromes are involved in diazepam metabolism, the impact of genetic variants in CYP2C19 appears to have a greater influence than CYP3A4 on diazepam plasma levels.

Several clinical studies have shown that individuals who are poor metabolizers (PM) for CYP2C19 have a lower plasma clearance of diazepam compared to normal or extensive metabolizers (EM), making plasma levels of diazepam in individuals who are poor metabolizers (PM) for CYP2C19 lower than in normal or extensive metabolizers (EM), making plasma levels of diazepam in individuals who are poor metabolizers (PM) for CYP2C19 lower.In PM, increased levels of diazepam in PM could affect response and increase the risk of adverse effects.

This information is crucial for understanding how genetic differences between patients may affect the efficacy and safety of diazepam treatment, even if it does not yet translate into specific changes in dosing guidelines.
707.technical_citations:
Dean L. Diazepam Therapy and CYP2C19 Genotype. 2018 Dec 10 [updated 2020 Oct 15]. In: Pratt VM, Scott SA, Pirmohamed M, Esquivel B, Kattman BL, Malheiro AJ, editors. Medical Genetics Summaries [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Center for Biotechnology Information (US); 2012-.

Fukasawa T, Suzuki A, Otani K.Effects of genetic polymorphism of cytochrome P450 enzymes on the pharmacokinetics of benzodiazepines. J Clin Pharm Ther. 2007 Aug;32(4):333-41.

Zubiaur P, Figueiredo-Tor L, Villapalos-García G, et al.Association between CYP2C19 and CYP2B6 phenotypes and the pharmacokinetics and safety of diazepam. Biomed Pharmacother. 2022 Nov;155:113747.

708.name: Iloperidone (Dosage)
708.description: Iloperidone is an atypical antipsychotic used in the treatment of schizophrenia. A relationship has been established between the efficacy of this drug and pharmacogenetics, which can determine the final plasma concentration of the metabolite.
708.overview:
Iloperidone is a second generation or atypical antipsychotic used to treat the symptoms of schizophrenia.

ACTION MECHANISM

The action mechanism of iloperidone is yet unknown. It is believed that the efficacy of iloperidone is mediated through a combination of the antagonism of dopamine type 2 receptors and serotonin type 2 receptors.

CAUTIONS

Is not recommended the prescription of iloperidone in patients with hepatic insufficiency

In elderly patients with psychosis related to dementia, there is an increased risk of mortality during treatment with iloperidone.

Iloperidone prolongs the cardiac QT interval, avoid supplying iloperidone with other substances that prolong the QT interval: class IA antiarrhythmics (such as quinidine, procainamide) or antiarrhythmics class III (such as amiodarone, sotalol), antiarrhythmic substances, antipsychotics (such as ziprasidone, chlorpromazine, thioridazine), antibiotics (such as gatifloxacin, moxifloxacin) or any other substance that prolongs the QTc interval (such as pentamidine, levomethadyl acetate, methadone).

Special caution should be taken in patients with a history of cardiac arrhythmias and congenital long QT syndrome. Extreme caution in circumstances that increase the risk of torsade de pointes, such as bradycardia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, congenital prolongation of the QT interval, recent acute myocardial infarction, decompensated heart failure.

In patients with hyperglycemia and diabetes mellitus, regular monitoring should be performed. This regular control should be extended to patients with risk factors for diabetes mellitus such as obesity, family history (fasting glucose control should be performed at the beginning of treatment with iloperidone and periodically during the duration of treatment).

Extreme caution in patients with a history of seizures or in conditions that potentially decrease the seizure threshold (Alzheimer's type dementia).

Iloperidone can affect judgment, thinking and motor skills. In patients at risk of suicide perform close supervision during treatment.

PHARMACOLOGIC INTERACTIONS

Plasma concentrations of iloperidone may be increased by: potent inhibitors of CYP3A4 (eg ketoconazole, itraconazole), potent inhibitors of CYP2D6 (eg paroxetine, fluoxetine).

SIDE EFFECTS

Palpitations; prolongation of the cardiac QT interval; conjunctivitis (including allergic); weight loss; myalgia, muscle spasms; restlessness, aggressiveness, delirium; urinary incontinence; erectile dysfunction.

BRAND NAME

Fanapt®
708.dose: The FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration) indicates that CYP2D6 poor metabolizers (PM) may have increased systemic concentrations and increased risk of adverse effects (QT interval prolongation) with iloperidone treatment compared to normal or extensive metabolizers (EM) CYP2D6. It recommends that the dose of iloperidone be reduced by 50% in CYP2D6 PM patients.
708.technical_description:
Iloperidone is a second-generation atypical antipsychotic that, like other drugs in its class (such as risperidone and aripiprazole), causes fewer extrapyramidal side effects than typical first-generation antipsychotics. However, it still poses risks of adverse effects such as cardiac QT interval lengthening, hyperprolactinemia, and metabolic problems, including diabetes, dyslipidemia, and weight changes.

Iloperidone is rapidly absorbed and metabolized in the liver primarily by cytochromes CYP2D6 and CYP3A4. Patients who have two inactive copies of the CYP2D6 gene are termed poor metabolizers (PM) and may have an impaired ability to metabolize iloperidone. These individuals may be at increased risk for adverse effects due to higher plasma iloperidone exposure, while having a more effective response (since drug exposure is much longer), compared to normal or extensive metabolizers (EM), who have two active copies of CYP2D6.

Patients who are co-medicated with CYP2D6 inhibitors (e.g. fluoxetine) while taking iloperidone should also have their dosage reduced.Patients who are co-medicated with CYP2D6 inhibitors (e.g. fluoxetine) should also have the dose of iloperidone reduced, as in this case also the blood levels of the drug will be elevated due to inhibition of the CYP2D6 enzyme.
708.technical_citations:
Brennan MD.Pharmacogenetics of second-generation antipsychotics. Pharmacogenomics. 2014 Apr;15(6):869-84.

Danek PJ, Wójcikowski J, Daniel WA. The atypical neuroleptics iloperidone and lurasidone inhibit human cytochrome P450 enzymes in vitro. Evaluation of potential metabolic interactions. Pharmacol Rep. 2020 Dec;72(6):1685-1694.

Potkin SG, Preskorn S, Hochfeld M, Meng X.A thorough QTc study of 3 doses of iloperidone including metabolic inhibition via CYP2D6 and/or CYP3A4 and a comparison to quetiapine and ziprasidone. J Clin Psychopharmacol. 2013 Feb;33(1):3-10.

709.name: Pitolisant (Dosage)
709.description: Pitolisant is a medication used to treat excessive daytime sleepiness in people with narcolepsy, with or without cataplexy. It acts as an inverse agonist of the histamine H₃ receptor, which increases the release of histamine in the brain and stimulates wakefulness.
709.overview:
Pitolisant is a drug that acts as an inverse agonist and antagonist of the histamine H3 receptor in the central nervous system. It is mainly used for the treatment of narcolepsy with or without cataplexy and has also shown efficacy in the treatment of excessive sleepiness associated with other sleep disorders, such as obstructive sleep apnea.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Pitolisant works by blocking H3 receptors in the brain, which increases histamine release. Histamine plays an important role in regulating wakefulness, so its increase helps to improve alertness in patients with excessive sleepiness. It has been evaluated as an alternative to traditional stimulants, with a lower risk of abuse.

Pitolisant may also modulate other neurotransmitter systems, increasing the release of acetylcholine, norepinephrine, and dopamine.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Treatment with pitolisant is contraindicated in people with hypersensitivity to the drug or any of its excipients. Its use is also contraindicated in people with severe hepatic impairment and renal insufficiency, as both conditions can increase the concentration of the drug in the blood, considerably increasing the risk of adverse effects. Pitolisant may prolong the QT interval, so it is contraindicated in patients with long QT syndrome. Its use is not recommended in patients with epilepsy as it could lower the seizure threshold, nor in pregnant women.

SIDE EFFECTS

The most commonly reported side effects are insomnia, headaches, nausea, anxiety and irritability, increased heart rate, and prolongation of the QT interval (therefore, monitoring is required in patients with cardiovascular risk).

INTERACTIONS

Caution should be exercised when pitolisant is administered with CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, or bupropion, among others) or CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir, clarithromycin, etc.). In these cases, the plasma concentration of pitolisant may be increased, so it may be necessary to adjust the dose of the drug.

When pitolisant is administered with CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, or St. John's wort) or CYP2D6 inducers (such as dexamethasone), it may be necessary to increase the dose of pitolisant to achieve the desired therapeutic effect.

In addition to the above, pitolisant may interact with drugs that prolong the QT interval (amiodarone, citalopram), stimulants (amphetamines, methylphenidate), benzodiazepines and CNS depressants, hormonal contraceptives, and alcohol.

BRAND NAMES

Wakix®
Ozawade®
709.dose:
Treatment with pitolisant usually starts, under general conditions, with a dose of 9mg/day, taken in the morning to avoid insomnia. This dose can be progressively increased up to 35.6mg/day depending on the patient's tolerance.

Currently, various regulatory bodies such as the FDA, EMA or HCSC include dose adjustment recommendations for individuals with a poor metabolizer profile for CYP2D6.

In individuals who are poor metabolizers for CYP2D6, a dose reduction of the drug is recommended, as these patients have a higher systemic concentration of pitolisant compared to normal metabolizers. It is recommended not to exceed the maximum dose of 17.8mg in these individuals.
709.technical_description:
Pitolisant is a drug used to treat excessive daytime sleepiness in adults with narcolepsy. It is an H3 receptor agonist, which increases the activity of histaminergic neurons, improving wakefulness. Specifically, it binds to H3 receptors, inhibits the binding of endogenous histamine to them, and also produces an opposite response to that of endogenous histamine when binding to the receptor, with the final consequence of enhancing the effect of endogenous histamine in the brain.

 Pitolisant is metabolized in the liver to inactive compounds by cytochrome CYP2D6 and, to a lesser extent, by CYP3A4.

 Various studies have shown that the metabolism of pitolisant can be affected by the presence of certain polymorphisms that affect the metabolizing profile of CYP2D6. It has been observed that individuals with a "Poor Metabolizer" profile, meaning carriers of two copies of a loss-of-function allele, show significantly higher plasma concentrations of pitolisant than normal metabolizers. In these cases, the risk of adverse effects is increased, and therefore, regulatory agencies such as the FDA (Food and Drugs Administration) or the EMA (European Medicines Agency) recommend reducing the dose by 50% in these patients.
709.technical_citations: Drugs@FDA. Drug Product pitolisant NDA 211150.  Pitolisant. European Medicines Agency.  Harwell V, Fasinu PS.  Pitolisant and Other Histamine-3 Receptor Antagonists-An Update on Therapeutic Potentials and Clinical Prospects. Medicines (Basel). 2020 Sep 1;7(9):55.

710.name: Fluindione (Adverse Reactions)
710.description: Fluindione is an oral anticoagulant that belongs to the vitamin K antagonist (VKA) class. It is used to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders, such as deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism.
710.overview:
Fluindione (marketed as Previscan®) is an anticoagulant with a half-life of approximately 30 hours, allowing it to remain in the body for an extended period after administration. It is commonly used in France, while other European countries tend to use other anticoagulants, such as acenocoumarol and phenprocoumon. Warfarin is more frequently used in North America, Scandinavia, the United Kingdom, and Asian countries.

Due to its narrow therapeutic range, it is essential to regularly monitor the International Normalized Ratio (INR) to adjust the VKA dose and ensure adequate anticoagulation, thus minimizing the risk of bleeding.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Its mechanism of action involves the inhibition of the synthesis of vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors in the liver, which reduces the formation of blood clots.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Fluindione is contraindicated in the following cases:

Allergy to fluindione or its components.
Pregnancy and lactation
Severe hepatic impairment
Use with other medications containing high doses of aspirin, phenylbutazone, miconazole (except for forms intended for cutaneous application), or St. John's wort.

SIDE EFFECTS

Bleeding is the most common adverse effect of fluindione and can manifest in different parts of the body, from minor bleeding to severe hemorrhages. The appearance of small purple spots on the skin due to microhemorrhages is also possible. Other adverse effects include gastrointestinal problems, hair loss, and hypersensitivity reactions that can affect the skin, liver, and kidneys.

INTERACTIONS

Fluindione can interact with various medications, which can increase or decrease its anticoagulant effect. Therefore, it is essential to inform the doctor about all medications and supplements being taken to prevent adverse interactions. Certain compounds, such as glucosamine used in the treatment of osteoarthritis, may enhance the effect of fluindione; therefore, patients undergoing treatment with both should be carefully monitored.

Like other anticoagulants, fluindione can also interact with certain foods. For example, garlic consumption could intensify its anticoagulant effect. Significant variations in the consumption of foods rich in vitamin K (such as broccoli, carrots, sauerkraut, cabbage, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, spinach, fennel, liver, lettuce, tomatoes, etc.) also influence the activity of these anticoagulants. Both excessive vitamin K intake and a drastic reduction in intake and prolonged fasting can affect the treatment.

TRADEMARKS

Previscan®
710.technical_description:
The enzyme encoded by the CYP2C9 gene participates in the hepatic metabolism of vitamin K antagonists, such as warfarin, acenocoumarol, and fluindione. Genetic variants that affect CYP2C9 activity can modify its metabolism in the liver. In Caucasian individuals, two genetic variants that reduce or abolish CYP2C9 function predominate: the rs1799853 polymorphism (CYP2C9*2 or c.430C>T) and rs1057910 (CYP2C9*3 or c.1075A>C). Individuals carrying alleles that reduce or inhibit CYP2C9 function have a higher risk of overexposure to VKAs and, therefore, a higher risk of bleeding compared to those with normal CYP2C9 function at standard doses.

Additionally, for these drugs, the gene with the greatest influence is VKORC1 (vitamin K epoxide reductase complex 1), which encodes a protein that is part of an enzymatic complex involved in the reduction of inactive vitamin K, producing active vitamin K, which is necessary for the activation of coagulation factors II, VII, IX, and X. The rs9923231 variant (c.-1639G>A) of the VKORC1 gene reduces its expression, and at standard doses of fluindione, there is a higher risk of bleeding than in individuals who do not have the variant.

It is important to consider that, in addition to genetics, there are other factors that can influence the risk of bleeding during treatment. These include smoking, age, ethnicity, diet, liver problems, and the concomitant use of other medications, such as statins, sulfonamides, amiodarone, and azole antifungals.
710.technical_citations:
Cullell N, Carrera C, Muiño E, et al. Pharmacogenetic studies with oral anticoagulants. Genome-wide association studies in vitamin K antagonist and direct oral anticoagulants. Oncotarget. 2018 Jun 26;9(49):29238-29258.
Lamoureux F, Duflot T; French Network of Pharmacogenetics (RNPGX). Pharmacogenetics in cardiovascular diseases: State of the art and implementation-recommendations of the French National Network of Pharmacogenetics (RNPGx). Therapie. 2017 Apr;72(2):257-267.

711.name: Floxacillin (Adverse effects)
711.description: Floxacillin is an antibiotic of the penicillin family used in the treatment of Gram-positive bacterial infections. Liver toxicity is one of the most serious adverse effects associated with the drug and certain genetic polymorphisms can increase the risk of presenting it.
711.technical_description:
Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) is a major cause of severe liver disease and an adverse reaction that can be induced by flucloxacillin, although its incidence is low (1-2 per 1000 individuals). The mechanisms that trigger this are not well understood, but it is thought to be multifactorial and influenced by the immune system and other factors involved in disease progression.

 Studies conducted to date have found a significant association between the HLA-B*57:01 allele and flucloxacillin-induced liver damage. HLA‑B*57:01 is an allelic variant of the major histocompatibility complex class I (HLA‑B), implicated in various adverse drug reactions. Carriers of this allele have been shown to have an 80-fold increased risk of developing this adverse effect compared to individuals who do not carry it.

 Immunological investigations have shown that, in carriers, after taking flucloxacillin, specific T lymphocytes are activated, which are responsible for the liver damage.

 Although the HLA-B*57:01 allele is the main genetic risk factor for developing DILI induced by flucloxacillin use, there are also other non-genetic factors that can influence the risk. These include being female, being elderly, and receiving flucloxacillin treatment for more than two weeks.

 The DPWG (Royal Dutch Pharmacists Association - Pharmacogenetics Working Group) clinical guideline recommends that when patients are HLA-B*57:01 positive, their liver function should be regularly monitored, and if liver enzyme levels and/or bilirubin are found to be elevated, an alternative drug should be selected.
711.technical_citations:
Ali SE, Waddington JC, Lister A, et al.Identification of flucloxacillin-modified hepatocellular proteins: implications in flucloxacillin-induced liver injury. Toxicol Sci. 2023 Mar 20;192(1):106-116.

Daly AK, Donaldson PT, Bhatnagar P, et al.HLA-B*5701 genotype is a major determinant of drug-induced liver injury due to flucloxacillin. Nat Genet. 2009 Jul;41(7):816-9.

Lindh M, Hallberg P, Yue QY, Wadelius M.Clinical factors predicting drug-induced liver injury due to flucloxacillin. Drug Healthc Patient Saf. 2018 Nov 21;10:95-101.

639.name: Thioguanine, Azathioprine, Mercaptopurine (Dosage)
639.description: The thiopurine family consists of purine metabolism antagonist drugs, widely used in the treatment of autoimmune diseases and some types of cancer. Thiopurine methyltransferase, encoded by the TPMT gene, is involved in their metabolism, so knowing the metabolizer profile can be useful for correct dosage adjustment and thus avoid the appearance of undesirable adverse effects.
639.overview:
Azathioprine

Azathioprine is an immunosuppressant derived from mercaptopurine that antagonizes purine metabolism. Its preferred use is in autoimmune diseases with insufficient response to corticosteroids, as well as in the prevention of rejection in transplant patients (associated with other immunosuppressants).

The most important side effects of azathioprine are bone marrow depression, gastrointestinal intolerance and hepatotoxicity. It should be used carefully in patients with gout or bacterial infections, and periodic hematological and biochemical controls should be performed.

Thioguanine

Thioguanine is an antineoplastic that also antagonizes purine metabolism. Upon activation it inhibits de novo purine synthesis as well as purine nucleotide interconversions. This drug is used in the treatment of acute leukemia.

The main side effects are related to gastrointestinal (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea), hepatobiliary (jaundice), metabolic (hyperuricemia) and hematological (myelosuppression) disorders.

Clinical implications

When there is some failure affecting these genes it can lead to TPMT deficiency, and with it, an abnormal metabolism of thiopurines. In most people this deficiency does not cause any health problems, since it is not very common to receive treatment with azathioprine or 6-mercaptopurine. However, those who receive these drugs and have TPMT deficiency metabolize these drugs more slowly and are exposed to more erratic responses and a higher rate of adverse effects. Some of these include nausea and increased transaminases, as well as others that can be serious, such as marrow suppression (inability to form new blood cells).

In addition to the TPMT gene, variations in the NUDT15 gene are associated with impaired metabolization of thiopurines and therefore present an increased risk of adverse reactions similar to those of the TPMT gene.
639.dose:
The CPIC (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium) guidelines recommend considering an alternative agent or extreme dose reduction of thioguanine, azathioprine, and mercaptopurine for patients who are poor metabolizers (PM) for TPMT or NUDT15. Consult the CPIC guideline by Relling et al. (2019) for more information.

Patients who are intermediate metabolizers for both TPMT and NUDT15 enzymes may also require substantial dose reduction to mitigate thiopurine treatment-induced toxicity (Mailland et al., 2024).
639.technical_description:
Several studies have reported that individuals carrying alleles that decrease the function of the TPMT and NUDT15 enzymes are unable to tolerate the usual doses of thiopurines.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) recommends genotyping of TPMT and NUDT15 before starting treatment with thioguanine, azathioprine and mercaptopurine. Multiple loss-of-function alleles have been described in both TPMT (e.g., TPMT*2 and TPMT*4) and NUDT15 (e.g., NUDT15*2 and NUDT15*3). Patients who carry 2 loss-of-function alleles for one of the two enzymes are considered poor metabolizers for the enzyme in question and may suffer severe adverse effects when treated with such drugs, due to high levels of thioguanine nucleotides that accumulate and become toxic.

TPMT and NUDT15 intermediate metabolizers are those with one normal function allele and one loss-of-function allele. If a patient is an intermediate metabolizer for either TPMT or NUDT15 they may also have an increased risk of adverse effects compared to normal metabolizers so it is generally recommended to reduce the initial dose.

In addition to the above alleles, there are other TPMT and NUDT15 alleles whose function is uncertain or unknown (e.g., TPMT*6, TPMT*8, TPMT*9, TPMT*12, TPMT*33, NUDT15*4 and NUDT15*6) and which, when present in combination with a TPMT or NUDT15 allele, may have a higher risk of adverse effects than normal metabolizers. We indicate in such cases that the patient is a "potential downstream metabolizer" for the corresponding enzyme. We indicate in these cases that the metabolizer status is "indeterminate" and this result should be evaluated by a specialist as the dose may also need to be reduced in these cases to minimize the risk of toxicity.
639.technical_citations:
Dickson AL, Daniel LL, Zanussi J, et al. TPMT and NUDT15 Variants Predict Discontinuation of Azathioprine for Myelotoxicity in Patients with Inflammatory Disease: Real-World Clinical Results. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2022 Jan;111(1):263-271.

Maillard M, Nishii R, Yang W, et al. Additive effects of TPMT and NUDT15 on thiopurine toxicity in children with acute lymphoblastic leukemia across multiethnic populations. J Natl Cancer Inst. 2024 May 8;116(5):702-710.

Relling MV, Schwab M, Whirl-Carrillo M, et al. Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium Guideline for Thiopurine Dosing Based on TPMT and NUDT15 Genotypes: 2018 Update. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2019 May;105(5):1095-1105.

179.name: Olanzapine (Efficacy)
179.description: Olanzapine is an atypical antipsychotic used in the treatment of schizophrenia, depressive episodes and bipolar disorder. The presence of polymorphisms in the dopamine receptor gene (DRD3) may affect the activity of olanzapine, and therefore its efficacy.
179.technical_description:
Olanzapine is transformed into its main metabolite, olanzapine-10-N-glucuronide, by the enzyme UGT1A4. However, other enzymes are involved in its transformation, one of the main ones being CYP1A2, while CYP2D6 plays a minor role. It has been observed that patients presenting two copies of the CYP1A2*1F allele which is defined by the presence of the -163C>A, c.-9-154C>A or rs762551 variant respond worse to olanzapine treatment than patients who do not carry the CYP1A2*1F allele.

Another gene that could be related to olanzapine response is ABCB1 or MDR1 which encodes for a transporter protein called P-gp or P-glycoprotein. This transporter is expressed in multiple tissues and transports xenobiotics out of the tissue or organ where it is expressed. Olanzapine is one of the substances it can transport and it has been shown that variants in the gene encoding it, such as rs1045642 or c.3435T>C can influence the response to treatment.

The Ser9Gly or rs6280 variant is a polymorphism located in the DRD3 gene (dopamine D3 receptor). It appears that olanzapine interacts with this receptor and therefore, variations in its sequence can affect the activity of olanzapine. It has been observed that the presence of the T or Ser allele is associated with a higher probability of worse response to olanzapine treatment and greater weight gain. In the study by Adams et al. it was observed that olanzapine-treated patients with CC genotype for rs6280 in the DRD3 gene more efficiently decreased their symptoms compared to patients with CT or TT genotypes.

The DRD2 gene encodes for the dopaminergic D2 receptor. Mutations in this gene have been associated with schizophrenia and also with response to olanzapine. One of the most studied variants is rs1799978 or A-241G, the presence of one copy of the C allele in patients with schizophrenia is associated with a faster response to olanzapine. Another well-studied variant in this same gene is a small deletion called rs1799732, c.-486_-485insC, or -141C Ins/Del that has been linked to a lower response to multiple antipsychotic drugs.

It should be kept in mind that the biomarkers analyzed here require confirmation in larger studies covering different populations and that, in addition, there are other factors that may interfere with response to olanzapine treatment. Therefore, these markers are not yet used in clinical practice.
179.technical_citations:
Adams DH, Close S, Farmen M, et al. Dopamine receptor D3 genotype association with greater acute positive symptom remission with olanzapine therapy in predominantly Caucasian patients with chronic schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder. Hum Psychopharmacol. 2008 Jun;23(4):267-74.

Czerwensky F, Leucht S, Steimer W. CYP1A2*1D and *1F polymorphisms have a significant impact on olanzapine serum concentrations. Ther Drug Monit. 2015 Apr;37(2):152-60.

Lencz T, Robinson DG, Xu K, et al. DRD2 promoter region variation as a predictor of sustained response to antipsychotic medication in first-episode schizophrenia patients. Am J Psychiatry. 2006 Mar;163(3):529-31.

Zubiaur P, Soria-Chacartegui P, Villapalos-García G, et al. The pharmacogenetics of treatment with olanzapine. Pharmacogenomics. 2021 Sep;22(14):939-958.

180.name: Olanzapine (Adverse effects)
180.description: Olanzapine is a medication belonging to the antipsychotic family used in the treatment of schizophrenia, manic episodes, or bipolar disorder. Weight gain, one of the main side effects, has been associated with the presence of certain polymorphic variants in genes that interact with olanzapine.
180.overview:
Olanzapine is in a class of medications called atypical antipsychotics, structurally similar to clozapine and having the same mechanism of action. Olanzapine binds to alpha-1, dopaminergic, histaminic H1, muscarinic and serotoninergic (type 5-HT2) receptors. Compared to the typical antipsychotics, olanzapine has more reduced cardiovascular effects and induces less hyperprolactinemia and extrapyramidal reactions. From a clinical point of view, olanzapine is effective in treating the symptoms of schizophrenia and is also used in treating acute mania with bipolar disorder, and in reducing agitation and other psychotic symptoms in dementia.

Olanzapine is an atypical neuroleptic with effects similar to other neuroleptics on is used to treat both negative and positive symptoms of schizophrenia, and is also effective for persistent positive symptoms. It works by changing the activity of certain natural substances in the brain.

Weight gain is one of the most important side effects associated with antipsychotic drug treatment. It has a negative influence on one's health (associated with diabetes, heart disease, and other serious disorders) and the personal well-being of the patient with a reduced fulfillment of treatment and the possible risk of recurrence of the illness. The importance of this problem was shown by Fontaine et al. who found that clozapine, in spite of preventing suicide in 492 out of 100,000 schizophrenic patients treated over a 10 year period, could also be considered responsible for 416 deaths caused by the consequences of weight gain induced by antipsychotics.

Among the atypical neuroleptic drugs, olanzapine and clozapine have been associated with a greater weight gain, with an average gain of 5 kg over short-term treatment and 6.7 kg over long-term treatment.

Genetic factors are believed to play an important role in an individual's response to antipsychotic drugs, both in the benefits of treatment and in the susceptibility to side effects. The variations in the gene sequence, called polymorphism, could influence the properties of the coded proteins and the results in individual response to the drugs.

If you wish to know all the substances that we analyze in our DNA test, please consult the pharmacological compatibility or pharmacogenetic section.
180.technical_description:
According to several studies, DRD2 has been shown to be an important gene in the development of schizophrenia. DRD2, encodes for the dopamine receptor type 2 which is a relevant presynaptic receptor. The rs1800497 variant, also known as c.2137G>A or Taq1A is located in the ANKK1(ankyrin repeat and kinase domain containing 1) gene. This variant is located 10 Kb from the DRD2 gene and has been associated with lower DRD2 expression. Taq1A has been studied for its importance in the response to antipsychotics and also for its association with substance abuse, alcohol dependence, eating disorders and smoking cessation.

The rs1800497 AA (ANKK1) genotype is associated with an increased risk of Headache when treated with olanzapine, quetiapine or risperidone in healthy individuals compared to the GG genotype (Lopez-Rodriguez et al., 2013). Another well-studied DRD2 variant is -141C Ins/Del or c.-486_-485insC (rs1799732) located in the 5′ promoter region of the gene. This polymorphism may influence the density of D2 receptors in the striatum of healthy volunteers not treated with antipsychotics. It has been observed, that schizophrenic patients carrying the Del allele might have a poor response to olanzapine and risperidone (Zhang and Malhotra. 2011).

Finally, we analyzed the rs7997012 variant of the HTR2A gene have also been correlated with antipsychotic treatment response and side effects.

It is important to take into consideration that there are other factors that may influence the response to olanzapine treatment. At present, the genetic variants analyzed here require further studies to confirm previous relationships before they can be employed in clinical practice.
180.technical_citations:
Alenius M, Wadelius M, Dahl ML, et al. Gene polymorphism influencing treatment response in psychotic patients in a naturalistic setting. J Psychiatr Res. 2008 Sep;42(11):884-93.

López-Rodríguez R, Cabaleiro T, Ochoa D, et al. Pharmacodynamic genetic variants related to antipsychotic adverse reactions in healthy volunteers. Pharmacogenomics. 2013 Jul;14(10):1203-14.

Zhang JP, Malhotra AK.Pharmacogenetics and antipsychotics: therapeutic efficacy and side effects prediction. Expert Opin Drug Metab Toxicol. 2011 Jan;7(1):9-37.

Zubiaur P, Soria-Chacartegui P, Villapalos-García G, et al. The pharmacogenetics of treatment with olanzapine. Pharmacogenomics. 2021 Sep;22(14):939-958.

452.name: Methotrexate in rheumatoid arthritis (Efficacy)
452.description: Methotrexate is a structural analog of folic acid widely used in the treatment of some types of cancer and rheumatic diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis. However, there is a high percentage of individuals who do not experience improvement with treatment, which could be associated with certain genetic markers.
452.overview:
Methotrexate is a drug pertaining to the antimetabolites family of drugs that presents several indications. It is used to treat rheumatoid arthritis, severe psoriasis, certain types of cancer (lymphomas, leukemias, uterus, breast, lung, head and neck) and is also used as an immunosuppressant in organ transplants to prevent rejection. Methotrexate can treat rheumatoid arthritis by decreasing the activity of the immune system. Methotrexate treats cancer by slowing the growth of cancer cells. Methotrexate treats psoriasis by slowing the growth of skin cells to interrupt desquamation. Methotrexate is used to prevent rejection of the graft in transplants since it decreases the activity of the immune system.

Methotrexate is also sometimes used to treat Crohn's disease, multiple sclerosis, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions that develop when the immune system attacks healthy cells in the body by mistake.

ACTION MECHANISM

Antineoplastic and immunosuppressant antagonist of folic acid. Methotrexate interferes in processes of DNA synthesis, repair and cellular replication; It can decrease the development of malignant tissues without irreversible damage in normal tissues.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Pregnancy, breastfeeding, existing or recent chickenpox, herpes zoster. Should be evaluated the risk-benefit ratio in patients suffering ascites, gastrointestinal obstruction, peritoneal or pleural effusion, renal dysfunction, depression of the bone marrow, history of gout, kidney stones, liver dysfunction, infection, peptic ulcer and ulcerative colitis. Caution should also be exercised in patients undergoing treatment with cytotoxic drugs or radiation therapy.

SIDE EFFECTS

Many adverse reactions are unavoidable and represent the pharmacological action of methotrexate; for example, leucopenia and thrombocytopenia, which are used as indicators of the effectiveness of the medication and facilitate the adjustment of the individual dosage. The development of side effects require medical attention only if they persist or are annoying: anorexia, nausea and vomiting. The side effects that are more frequent and require medical attention are: hematemesis, diarrhea, hematuria, arthralgia and edema. With intrathecal administration may appear: blurred vision, confusion, dizziness, drowsiness, headaches, seizures or unusual fatigue. Alopecia does not require medical attention. The development of certain side effects can indicate possible toxicity of the central nervous system: blurred vision, confusion, dizziness and headaches. Side effects are more likely to occur in very young or elderly patients.

When used as an antineoplastic the side effects that can be developed are the following: Leukocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia; headache, fatigue, drowsiness, paresthesia; pulmonary complications due to interstitial alveolitis/pneumonitis (typical symptoms: malaise, irritating and dry cough, respiratory distress that progresses to dyspnea at rest, chest pain, fever); loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, inflammation and ulceration of the mucous membrane of the mouth and throat, stomatitis, dyspepsia, diarrhea; rash, erythema, pruritus; increase in liver enzymes (ALAT, ASAT, alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin).

If an overdose of methotrexate occurs, it should be rescued with folinic acid by intravenous infusion in doses of up to 75 mg in the first 12 hours followed by folinic acid 15 mg oral or intravenous every 6 hours at least 4 doses, until the serum levels of methotrexate are less than 5x10-7 M. A treatment with intravenous and intrathecal carboxypeptidase G2 has been published to accelerate the clearance of methotrexate.

PHARMACOLOGIC INTERACTIONS

The simultaneous administration of intrathecal methotrexate with acyclovir can produce neurological abnormalities. Methotrexate increases the risk of hepatotoxicity with alcohol or hepatotoxic medications. Methotrexate increases the concentration of uric acid in the blood, so it is necessary to readjust the dosage of allopurinol, colchicine or probenecid. Methotrexate increases the anticoagulant activity and the risk of hemorrhages by reducing the hepatic synthesis of the coagulation factors, for this reason the dose of anticoagulants derived from coumarin needs to be regulated. The use of NSAIDs increases the risk of agranulocytosis (phenylbutazone). Asparaginase can block the effects of methotrexate by inhibiting cell replication. The administration of cytarabine after initiation of therapy with methotrexate may produce a synergistic cytotoxic effect. Folic acid can interfere with the antifolate effects of methotrexate. Oral kanamycin increases the absorption of oral methotrexate. Oral neomycin decreases the absorption of oral methotrexate. Probenecid and salicylates can inhibit renal excretion of methotrexate. Pyrimethamine, triamterene or trimethoprim may increase the toxic effects of methotrexate due to their similar actions as folic acid antagonists.

If you wish to know all the substances that we analyze in our DNA test, please consult the pharmacological compatibility or pharmacogenetic section.

BRAND NAMES

Rheumatrex®
Trexall®
Bertanel®
Metoject®
Emthexate®
452.technical_description:
Methotrexate is the most frequently prescribed antirheumatic drug to treat the symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, being even prescribed as monotherapy in many cases. Methotrexate enters cells and glutamic acid residues are added to it, forming methotrexate polyglutamate which promotes inhibition of the methionine, folate and adenosine pathways. It promotes the synthesis of pririmidines and purines that are essential for exerting the antiproliferative and anti-inflammatory properties of methotrexate.

Although methotrexate is well tolerated by patients, it is estimated that one third of patients do not respond well, due to insufficient efficacy or adverse effects. This lack of response may be due, in part, to variations in certain genes of folate metabolism or other genes, and a large number of pharmacogenetic studies are being carried out to obtain markers of response to methotrexate, without much success as yet.

The SLC19A1 (Solute Carrier Family 19 Member 1) gene encoding for reduced folate transporter 1 is responsible for the transport of methotrexate into cells. The rs1051266 variant of SLC19A1 is the most studied and patients with the TT genotype have been observed to have an increased response to the drug compared to CC and CT genotypes (Chen et al., 2017). It is believed that certain variants of SLC19A1 may influence transporter function and entry of methotrexate into cells thereby affecting treatment.

Another gene of interest in the response to methotrexate is ATIC (aminoimidazole carboxamide adenosine ribonucleotide transformylase) which codes for an enzyme involved in purine synthesis. Methotrexate polyglutamate directly inhibits the ATIC enzyme. One of the ATIC variants associated with methotrexate response is rs4673993 which is located in an intron and is thought to interfere with gene transcription. Patients with CC genotype have a greater response to methotrexate compared to patients with CT and TT genotypes (Lima et al., 2014).

It should be taken into consideration that, these variants alone cannot explain the variability in drug response observed between patients so other genes and associated factors are believed to be involved. In addition, further large studies in different populations are required to confirm both associations.
452.technical_citations:
Abdallah HY, Ibrahim ME, Abd El-Fadeal NM, et al. Pharmacogenomics of Methotrexate Pathway in Rheumatoid Arthritis Patients: Approach toward Personalized Medicine. Diagnostics (Basel). 2022 Jun 27;12(7):1560.

Chen Y, Zou K, Sun J, et al. Are gene polymorphisms related to treatment outcomes of methotrexate in patients with rheumatoid arthritis? A systematic review and meta-analysis. Pharmacogenomics. 2017 Jan;18(2):175-195.

Lee YH, Song GG. A meta-analysis of the association between the ATIC 347 C/G polymorphism and methotrexate responsiveness and toxicity in rheumatoid arthritis. Semin Arthritis Rheum. 2024 Feb;64:152337.

Lima A, Bernardes M, Azevedo R, et al. Moving toward personalized medicine in rheumatoid arthritis: SNPs in methotrexate intracellular pathways are associated with methotrexate therapeutic outcome. Pharmacogenomics. 2016 Oct;17(15):1649-1674.

619.name: Isoniazid (Adverse effects)
619.description: Isoniazid is an antibiotic used in the treatment of tuberculosis, acting as a potent bactericidal agent of Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Its metabolism, especially related to NAT2 (N-acetyltransferase 2) genotype, may influence its efficacy and risk of adverse effects, such as hepatotoxicity.
619.overview:
Tuberculosis is one of the infectious diseases that causes the most deaths in the world, about 1.6 million annually. Treatments with first-line anti-tuberculosis drugs such as rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol are generally effective, however, the development of drug resistance has significantly affected the success of this treatment.

Introduced in 1952, isoniazid is characterized by its good tolerability and low cost. As previously described, antituberculosis drugs cause the death of the infectious agent and therefore have a bactericidal effect. In the case of isoniazid, the drug has a high early bactericidal activity, reducing the bacterial load by 90-95% in the first couple of days of treatment.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Isoniazid is an antibiotic used mainly in the treatment and prevention of tuberculosis. This drug, one of the most effective first-line drugs against Mycobacterium tuberculosis, inhibits the synthesis of mycolic acid, an essential component of the bacterium's cell wall, resulting in its death.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Isoniazid should not be used in patients with known hypersensitivity to the active ingredient or to any of its excipients. It should also not be used in patients with severe hepatic insufficiency.

This drug shows undesirable interactions with carbamazepine and disulfiram, so their simultaneous use is not recommended. In addition, caution is recommended in case of combining isoniazid with other compounds such as halogenated volatile anesthetics, coumarin anticoagulants, ketoconazole, glucocorticoids, phenytoin or rifampicin, among others.

SIDE EFFECTS

Although isoniazid is generally safe, it can cause adverse reactions, most of which are transient and low-grade. These include gastrointestinal effects, skin rashes and pruritus, and peripheral neuropathy, although the incidence of the latter is very low. However, one of the most serious adverse effects associated with antituberculosis drugs is hepatotoxicity, which can lead to treatment failure and increased mortality in patients with tuberculosis.

Isoniazid is metabolized in the liver primarily through acetylation by the enzyme N-acetyl transferase 2 (NAT2). The NAT2 gene, which encodes this enzyme, is highly polymorphic and shows wide variations in its expression and enzymatic activity among different individuals. Some genetic polymorphisms in the NAT2 gene have been associated with an increased plasma concentration of isoniazid and an increased risk of developing hepatotoxicity.

BRAND NAMES

Isoniazid is marketed under a multitude of names, including:

Isonarif®
Isaotamine®
Rifamate®
Rifater®
619.technical_description:
Isoniazid is a first-line antibiotic used in the treatment of active tuberculosis caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Isoniazid acts as a prodrug that, when activated by the enzyme catalase-peroxidase KatG, produces radicals that inhibit the synthesis of mycolic acids, essential components of the bacterial cell wall. The combination of isoniazid with other antituberculosis drugs enhances its effectiveness.

The metabolism of isoniazid takes place mainly in the liver, where the enzyme N-acetyltransferase 2 (NAT2) converts isoniazid to acetylisoniazid (AcINH), which is subsequently hydrolyzed to acetylhydrazine. In addition, isoniazid can be hydrolyzed directly to hydrazine, a potent hepatotoxin. NAT2 also metabolizes hydrazine to acetylhydrazine and finally to diacetylhydrazine, a non-toxic compound. Variations in isoniazid metabolism are strongly influenced by genetic polymorphisms in the NAT2 gene, resulting in three distinct phenotypes: fast, intermediate and slow acetylators.

The influence of polymorphisms in NAT2 is critical in the pharmacokinetics of isoniazid and susceptibility to its adverse effects, especially hepatotoxicity. Slow acetylators(NAT2*5, *6, *7 and *14) have a higher plasma concentration of isoniazid due to their lower metabolizing capacity, which significantly increases the risk of hepatotoxicity. In contrast, rapid acetylators (NAT2*4) rapidly convert isoniazid to its non-toxic metabolites, thus decreasing the risk of hepatotoxicity and correlating the NAT2*4 haplotype with protection against this adverse effect. However, rapid drug elimination in rapid acetylators may also result in sub-therapeutic levels, negatively affecting treatment efficacy.

Therefore, knowing the NAT2 genotype allows optimizing the efficacy of isoniazid treatment and minimizing the incidence of serious adverse effects in high-mortality diseases such as tuberculosis.
619.technical_citations:
O'Connor C, Patel P, Brady MF. Isoniazid. 2024 Feb 16. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–. PMID: 32491549.

 Pourmohamadi N, et al. Association of Cytochrome P450 2E1 and N-Acetyltransferase 2 Genotypes with Serum Isoniazid Level and Anti-Tuberculosis Drug-Induced Hepatotoxicity: A Cross-Sectional Study. Iran J Med Sci. 2023 Sep;48(5):474-483.

 Sileshi T, Telele NF, Burkley V, et al. Correlation of N-acetyltransferase 2 genotype and acetylation status with plasma isoniazid concentration and its metabolic ratio in ethiopian tuberculosis patients. Sci Rep. 2023 Jul 15;13(1):11438.

 Verma R, da Silva KE, Rockwood N, et al. A Nanopore sequencing-based pharmacogenomic panel to personalize tuberculosis drug dosing. medRxiv [Preprint]. 2023 Sep 10:2023.09.08.23295248.

469.name: Amifampridine (Dosage)
469.description: Amifampridine is a potassium channel inhibitor drug used in the treatment of myasthenic syndromes such as Lambert-Eaton syndrome. Polymorphisms in the NAT2 gene can determine the plasma concentrations of the drug and its genotyping can be useful for the adjustment of the appropriate dose.
469.overview:
Amifampridine is a drug that acts on the nervous system for the symptomatic treatment of Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Amifampridine blocks voltage-dependent potassium channels by prolonging the depolarization of the presynaptic cell membrane, thus promoting calcium entry into the nerve ending. The resulting increase in intracellular calcium concentrations facilitates exocytosis of acetylcholine-containing vesicles, which in turn increases neuromuscular transmission. The result is an increase in muscle strength and resting compound muscle action potential amplitudes.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Hypersensitivity to amifampridine; epileptic patients; patients with uncontrolled asthma; patients with congenital cardiac QT interval syndromes.

Amifampridine is contraindicated in concomitance with sultopride, with drugs that can prolong QTc and with drugs with narrow therapeutic margin.

Do not use during pregnancy. Not for use during lactation since it is not known whether amifampridine is excreted in human breast milk.

PRECAUTIONS

Extreme caution should be exercised when amifampridine is prescribed in patients with hepatic or renal insufficiency. Caution in patients at risk of epileptic seizures: if a seizure occurs immediately discontinue treatment with amifampridine. A clinical and electrocardiographic control should always be performed at the beginning of treatment and once a year thereafter. Amifampridine is not indicated to treat children under 18 years of age since there are no safety and efficacy data.

SIDE EFFECTS

Peripheral and peribuccal paresthesias; epigastralgia, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain; sleep disorders; convulsions, anxiety, dizziness, weakness, tiredness, headache, chorea, myoclonus; blurred vision; heart rhythm disturbances, palpitations; Raynaud's syndrome, cold extremities; cough, bronchial hypersecretion, asthma attacks in asthmatics or with a history of asthma; elevation of transaminases.

PHARMACOLOGICAL INTERACTIONS

Amifampridine increases the risk of seizures with: antidepressants (tricyclics, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors), neuroleptics (phenothiazines and butyrophenones), mefloquine, bupropion, tramadol.

Amifampridine increases the effect of direct or indirect cholinesterase inhibitors, and decreases the effect of both inhibitors if amifampridine is taken concomitantly with: mivacurium, pipercurium, suxamethonium. Extreme precautions should be taken when administered concomitantly with drugs that are eliminated by metabolism or active secretion.

BRAND NAMES

Firdapse®
Rusurgui®
469.dose: The FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration) indicates in the package insert of Rusurgui®(amifampiridine) that poor NAT2 metabolizers may present an increase in the systemic concentration of amifampiridine and a higher risk of adverse effects. They indicate that in NAT2 poor metabolizers, the lowest recommended starting dose should be used and adverse reactions should be monitored.
469.technical_description:
N-acetyltransferases, such as NAT2, are enzymes that act primarily in the liver to detoxify a large number of chemicals, including some drugs such as amifampridine.

Variants in the NAT2 gene that can affect NAT2 acetylation function are important because of the role they play in the activation and/or deactivation of many drugs and chemicals, including those produced by tobacco, as well as drugs composed of aromatic amines and hydrazine that are used medicinally. In turn, the presence of deleterious polymorphisms in NAT2 can affect an individual's risk of cancer.

Certain variants in the NAT2 gene can affect the rate and extent of amifampridine metabolism. NAT2 poor metabolizers, known as slow acetylators, are those carrying two alleles with reduced function. In slow acetylators the drug concentrations in the body are higher than in intermediate and fast acetylators, with one and two functional alleles, respectively. Therefore, slow NAT2 acetylators have a higher risk of adverse effects if treated with amifampridine.

In the general population, the prevalence of poor metabolizers for NAT2 is 40-60% in Caucasian and African-American populations, and 10-30% in Asian populations.
469.technical_citations: Kuznetsov IB, McDuffie M, Moslehi R. A web server for inferring the human N-acetyltransferase-2 (NAT2) enzymatic phenotype from NAT2 genotype. Bioinformatics. 2009 May 1;25(9):1185-6.

328.name: Valproic Acid (Adverse effects)
328.description: Valproic acid is an antiepileptic and mood stabilizing drug used for the treatment of epilepsy and bipolar disorder. Polymorphisms in the POLG gene have been associated with an increase in associated hepatic toxic reactions.
328.overview:
Valproic acid (VPA), also known as sodium valproate, magnesium valproate, or simply valproate, is an antiepileptic drug and mood stabilizer used in the treatment of conditions such as epilepsy or bipolar disorder. VPA increases brain GABA levels, decreases excitatory amino acid levels, and modifies potassium conductance.

Valproic acid is used to treat disorders such as epilepsy and bipolar disorder. Although it is very effective in controlling seizures, its use must be carefully monitored due to side effects and interactions with other medications.

If you wish to know all the substances we analyze in our DNA test, consult the drug compatibility section or pharmacogenomics.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Hypersensitivity to sodium valproate.

Acute hepatitis, chronic hepatitis, personal or family history of severe hepatitis, hepatic porphyria, previous or current liver disease and/or severe liver or pancreatic dysfunction, branched-chain amino acid or urea cycle metabolism disorders, known mitochondrial disorders caused by mutations in the nuclear gene encoding the mitochondrial enzyme polymerase gamma, such as Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome. It is contraindicated in children under 2 years of age suspected of having a polymerase gamma-related disorder.

PRECAUTIONS

Renal insufficiency. In systemic lupus erythematosus, assess benefit-risk. Severe liver failure has been reported, with maximum risk in infants and children under 3 years of age with brain injury, mental retardation, or metabolic or genetic degenerative disease: evaluate liver function before starting treatment and monitor it periodically during the first 6 months of treatment.

In children under 3 years of age, monotherapy is recommended, and concomitant use with salicylates should be avoided due to the risk of liver toxicity. Discontinue in case of pancreatitis. Perform hematological tests before starting treatment, before surgical intervention, and in case of spontaneous bruises or hemorrhages.

Do not consume alcohol. In case of suspected urea cycle enzyme deficiency, perform metabolic examinations.

Possible weight gain at the start of treatment. Changes in treatment or gradual discontinuation.

Do not administer to girls, adolescent women, women of childbearing age, and pregnant women unless alternative treatments are not effective or tolerated; the teratogenic potential and the risk of developing developmental disorders in children in utero are high. Women of childbearing potential must use an effective contraceptive method, and the risks in case of pregnancy should be explained to them. If pregnancy occurs, an assessment of the benefits and risks of continuing treatment should be made, considering other therapeutic alternatives.

Monitor for signs of suicidal ideation and behavior.

In patients suspected of or presenting with mitochondrial disease (clinical signs of these underlying mitochondrial diseases caused by mitochondrial DNA mutations, as well as the nuclear gene encoding polymerase gamma, may be triggered or worsened), perform polymerase gamma mutation tests in accordance with current clinical practice for the diagnostic evaluation of such disorders.

SIDE EFFECTS

Valproic acid can cause anemia, thrombocytopenia, weight gain, tremor, extrapyramidal disorders, stupor, somnolence, seizures, memory failure, headache, nystagmus, dizziness after injection, deafness, nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea, hypersensitivity, alopecia (transient, dose-related), hyponatremia, hemorrhage, liver injury, dysmenorrhea, confusional state, aggression, agitation, and attention disorders. In some cases, cognitive function may be impaired, especially with long-term treatment.

DRUG INTERACTIONS

VPA potentiates the effect of neuroleptics, MAOIs, antidepressants, and benzodiazepines.

VPA intake increases plasma concentrations of phenobarbital, free phenytoin, primidone, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, zidovudine, nimodipine, and ethosuximide.

Serum concentrations of valproic acid are decreased by: phenytoin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine, and carbapenems.

Serum concentrations of valproic acid are increased by: felbamate, cimetidine, fluoxetine, and erythromycin.

VPA decreases the clearance of felbamate. There is a risk of hemorrhage if prescribed concomitantly with anticoagulants and ASA.

The antibiotic mefloquine is contraindicated with the use of valproic acid due to the risk of seizures, which can aggravate the patient's condition.

Liver toxicity exacerbated by alcohol.

Adjust dose if prescribed concomitantly with rifampicin.

Risk of encephalopathy and/or hyperammonemia if taken concomitantly with topiramate.

VPA increases propofol exposure, so it is recommended to reduce the propofol dose during valproic treatment.

VPA intake may give false positives in ketone body excretion tests.

BRAND NAMES

Depakine®Valproic acid®.
328.technical_description:
VPA is one of the most widely used antiepileptic drugs worldwide to treat the manic phases of bipolar disorder and also in other psychiatric disorders. Recently, VPA, alone or in combination with other anti-tumor drugs, has started to be used for the treatment of several cancers. It has also been shown that it may have a neuroprotective effect in patients with Alzheimer's disease.

Although VPA is generally well tolerated, it has been observed that exceptionally it can produce severe hepatotoxicity. This adverse effect has been associated with the presence of variants in the POLG gene that codes for the catalytic subunit of mitochondrial DNA polymerase.

More than 150 different pathogenic variants have been described in POLG that can produce multiple clinical phenotypes such as Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome. Among them, the most frequent is rs113994095, also called c.1399G> A or A467T, which significantly reduces enzyme activity to only 4% of normal activity. Another variant that markedly decreases activity is rs113994097, c.2243G>C or W748S.

Stewart et al. conducted a prospective study in patients from different centers in the United States included in the Drug Induced Liver Injury Network (DILIN) from 2004 to 2008. This study demonstrated that the presence of a single copy of a pathogenic variant in POLG could lead to liver damage during treatment with VPA.

When there is suspicion that a patient may have a mitochondrial disease (e.g. Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome, autosomal recessive inheritance) caused by mutations in POLG, the gene should be analyzed before VPA is used. Treatment with VPA is contraindicated in these patients.

The detection of variants in the POLG gene can be helpful in deciding whether to use VPA as an antiepileptic or bipolar disorder treatment, as well as in adjusting the dose of VPA in patients with prolonged treatment while minimizing the risk of liver damage. Recent studies, such as the one carried out by Harju et al. suggest that it could also be of interest to know whether a patient carries pathogenic variants in POLG in therapies combining VPA with antitumor drugs.
328.technical_citations: Harju T, Hurme-Niiranen A, Suo-Palosaari M, et al. DNA polymerase gamma variants and hepatotoxicity during maintenance therapy of childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia: is there a causal relationship? Pharmacogenomics J. 2023 Sep;23(5):105-111.Saneto RP, Lee IC, Koenig MK, et al. POLG DNA testing as an emerging standard of care before instituting valproic acid therapy for pediatric seizure disorders. Seizure. 2010 Apr;19(3):140-6.Sitarz KS, Elliott HR, Karaman BS, et al.Valproic acid triggers increased mitochondrial biogenesis in POLG-deficient fibroblasts. Mol Genet Metab. 2014 May;112(1):57-63.Stewart JD, Horvath R, Baruffini E, et al. Polymerase γ gene POLG determines the risk of sodium valproate-induced liver toxicity. Hepatology. 2010 Nov;52(5):1791-6.

624.name: Daunorubicin, Doxorubicin (Adverse Reactions)
624.description: The most severe adverse reaction caused by the use of anthracyclines as chemotherapeutic agents is cardiotoxicity, which can lead to cardiac dysfunction and heart failure. Variants of the ARG, UGT1A6, and SLC28A3 genes influence the risk of suffering this adverse effect, and their analysis is becoming increasingly relevant when assessing the most suitable treatment for each patient.
624.overview:
Anthracyclines have been used for over 60 years in cancer treatment and have saved many lives. However, a significant limitation of their use is cardiotoxicity.

Daunorubicin is an anthracycline used to treat acute myeloid leukemia and acute lymphoid leukemia in children, although it can also be used in adult patients.

Doxorubicin is used to treat certain types of cancer of the urinary bladder, breast, lung, stomach, and ovary, Ewing's sarcoma, Hodgkin's lymphoma (Hodgkin's disease) and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, Wilms' tumor, thyroid cancer, neuroblastoma, and certain leukemias.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

Anthracyclines alter DNA by interfering with topoisomerase, an enzyme essential for DNA replication and synthesis, thereby causing cell cycle arrest and programmed cell death.

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Treatment with anthracyclines is contraindicated in cases of hypersensitivity to anthracyclines and anthracenediones, persistent myelosuppression, severe infections, severe hepatic impairment, heart failure, recent myocardial infarction, severe arrhythmias, previous treatments with the same or another cardiotoxic anthracycline, pregnancy, and lactation.

SIDE EFFECTS

Nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain
Mouth and throat sores
Weight loss or gain
Muscle weakness
Hair loss
Tingling in the hands and feet

You should contact your doctor immediately if you develop hives, rash, itching, difficulty breathing or swallowing, or seizures.

As mentioned, cardiotoxicity is an adverse effect that limits its use and can occur acutely (during treatment), early (during the first year of treatment), or late (years or decades later). Therefore, close monitoring of patients should be carried out before, during, and after cancer therapy. The determination of LVEF (left ventricular ejection fraction) by echocardiography or with the technique of multiple-gated acquisition (MUGA) scan and magnetic resonance imaging are tests that are performed to assess for cardiotoxicity. Biochemical markers that can be used for early detection of cardiac damage are troponin T and N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP).

BRAND NAMES

Adriamycin®
Cerubidine®
Doxil®
Myocet®
Rubex®
Vyxeos®
624.technical_description:
There is clear evidence of the role of pharmacogenetics and pharmacogenomics in susceptibility to cardiotoxicity induced by drugs used for cancer treatment. The most studied are the anthracyclines daunorubicin and doxorubicin.

Cardiotoxic effects can range from coronary heart disease, pericardial disease, valvular heart disease, cardiomyopathy, and arrhythmias. Congestive heart failure (also called heart failure) is the main cardiomyopathy induced by these chemotherapeutic agents.

In addition to genetics, there are other factors that can influence the risk of anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity, which have a cumulative effect, such as concomitant treatment with radiotherapy, high doses of anthracyclines, shorter intravenous perfusion time, the presence of cardiovascular diseases, dyslipidemia, age over 65 years and female sex.

Currently, the CPNDS (Canadian Pharmacogenomics Network for Drug Safety) pharmacogenomic clinical guideline recommends genotyping of the three variants RARG rs2229774, SLC28A3 rs7853758 and UGT1A6*4 (rs17863783).

Increased RARG activity decreases the expression of the topoisomerase II gene (TOP2B), and it has been shown that TOP2B reduction has a cardioprotective effect. The RARG rs2229774 variant reduces the ability of RARG to decrease TOP2B expression, which negatively affects cardioprotection.

The SLC28A3 gene encodes an anthracycline transporter protein, the SLC28A3 rs7853758 variant produces a decrease in gene expression, thereby reducing the entry of anthracyclines into cardiac cells. Therefore, the rs7853758 variant is considered to have a protective effect against anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity.

UGT1A6 is a UDP-glucuronosyltransferase enzyme that participates in the detoxification of drugs and other xenobiotics. The rs17863783 variant represents the UGT1A6*4 allele, which is an allele that reduces UGT1A6-mediated glucuronidation activity. It has been observed that the presence of this variant increases the amount of cardiotoxic metabolites, thereby increasing the likelihood of anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity.
624.technical_citations:
Aminkeng F, Ross CJ, Rassekh SR, et al. Recommendations for genetic testing to reduce the incidence of anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2016 Sep;82(3):683-95.
Antoniadi K, Thomaidis N, Nihoyannopoulos P, et al. Prognostic Factors for Cardiotoxicity among Children with Cancer: Definition, Causes, and Diagnosis with Omics Technologies. Diagnostics (Basel). 2023 May 26;13(11):1864.
Chang VY, Wang JJ. Pharmacogenetics of Chemotherapy-Induced Cardiotoxicity. Curr Oncol Rep. 2018 Apr 30;20(7):52.

623.name: Cisplatin (Adverse Reactions)
623.description: Cisplatin is an alkylating agent widely used in chemotherapy against different types of cancer in both children and adults. The TPMT enzyme is involved in its metabolism, so polymorphisms in this gene are associated with adverse treatment reactions such as hearing loss.
623.overview:
Cisplatin, or cis-diamminedichloroplatinum(II), is a well-known metal-based chemotherapeutic drug used in the standard treatment of various solid cancers such as testicular, breast, ovarian, head and neck, bladder, lung, cervical, stomach, melanoma, neuroblastoma, osteosarcoma, and lymphomas. Depending on the type of cancer, cisplatin can be used alone or with other compounds such as, for example, paclitaxel, in the treatment of ovarian cancer.

Two of the main limitations of cisplatin use are treatment resistance and toxicity. Multiple platinum compounds have been produced to minimize the development of treatment resistance and improve pharmacokinetic properties. Some of the platinum compounds alternative to cisplatin are carboplatin, oxaliplatin, satraplatin, and nedaplatin.

The main toxicities derived from cisplatin treatment are nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, hepatotoxicity, gastrointestinal toxicity, and neurotoxicity.

MECHANISM OF ACTION

The main mechanism through which cisplatin exerts its action is the interaction with genomic and mitochondrial DNA and the generation of damage in its structure, activating apoptosis (cell death).

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Cisplatin should be avoided in patients with hypersensitivity to cisplatin or other platinum-based compounds, renal dysfunction, dehydration, myelosuppression, hearing impairment, or neuropathy caused by cisplatin.

SIDE EFFECTS

Cisplatin is a medication that can cause toxicity and multiple adverse effects including: infections, sepsis, hematological disorders (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anemia), low blood sodium levels, venous thromboembolism, peripheral neuropathy, hearing loss, vertigo, cardiac arrhythmia, erythema, skin ulcer, and localized edema.

Cisplatin ototoxicity is one of the most frequent adverse reactions, manifesting as permanent bilateral hearing loss, affecting 10-25% of adults and 26-90% of children.

TRADE NAMES

Platinol®
Platinol-AQ®
623.technical_description:
It is believed that the adverse effect of cisplatin-induced hearing loss is due to its interference with signal transduction in the cochlea, which is part of the inner ear. Studies suggest that cisplatin could cause apoptosis in the outer hair cells of the organ of Corti, the spiral ganglion, and the stria vascularis. However, the underlying mechanism that triggers apoptosis in hair cells is not fully understood.

Certain factors can influence the risk of developing cisplatin-induced ototoxicity, such as cisplatin dosage, age, presence of pre-existing hearing impairments, use alongside aminoglycosides, and cranial irradiation. These factors have a cumulative effect on the risk. In addition, variation in genes involved in the metabolism, transport, and targets of cisplatin has been observed to be another important factor.

Thiopurine S-methyltransferase (TPMT) is an enzyme that plays a fundamental role in the metabolism of cisplatin and other chemotherapeutic drugs. Studies have shown that genetic polymorphisms in TPMT contribute to the risk of cisplatin-induced ototoxicity in children. Patients carrying at least one loss-of-function TPMT allele respond worse to cisplatin chemotherapy than those patients carrying both functional alleles.

Currently, the CPNDS (Canadian Pharmacogenomics Network for Drug Safety) pharmacogenomic clinical guideline recommends genotyping for 3 of the TPMT variants that define the loss-of-function alleles *3A, *3B, *3C, and the loss-of-function allele TPMT*2 in pediatric patients who are to be treated with cisplatin. The study of these variants explains approximately 25% of cases of cisplatin-induced ototoxicity and has a high positive predictive value (92%). Other less frequent variants in TPMT have been described that could influence its enzymatic activity and are not included in the CPNDS guideline recommendations or in this analysis.
623.technical_citations:
Ghosh S. Cisplatin: The first metal based anticancer drug. Bioorg Chem. 2019 Jul;88:102925. 
 Lee JW, Pussegoda K, Rassekh SR, et al. Clinical Practice Recommendations for the Management and Prevention of Cisplatin-Induced Hearing Loss Using Pharmacogenetic Markers. Ther Drug Monit. 2016 Aug;38(4):423-31.
 Thiesen S, Yin P, Jorgensen AL, et al. TPMT, COMT and ACYP2 genetic variants in paediatric cancer patients with cisplatin-induced ototoxicity. Pharmacogenet Genomics. 2017 Jun;27(6):213-222.

632.name: Mivacurium and succinylcholine (Adverse Reactions)
632.description: Pseudocholinesterase deficiency is a rare enzymatic alteration that leads to an increased risk of adverse effects from certain muscle relaxants, such as mivacurium and succinylcholine, used during general anesthesia in certain countries.
632.overview:
Mivacurium is a short-acting, non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agent used as an adjunct in general anesthesia to relax skeletal muscles. This medication works by competitively binding to cholinergic receptors at the motor endplate, blocking the action of acetylcholine, and consequently inhibiting neuromuscular transmission.

Meanwhile, succinylcholine (or suxamethonium) is an ultra-short-acting muscle relaxant belonging to the class of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents, mainly used to facilitate tracheal intubation and muscle relaxation in surgical procedures.

Both mivacurium and succinylcholine are rapidly metabolized by enzymatic hydrolysis catalyzed by the enzyme pseudocholinesterase or butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) in plasma. However, a rare but potentially serious adverse effect is prolonged apnea after administration of these agents under general anesthesia. This risk is associated with defects in the BChE enzyme, which may be caused by mutations in the BCHE gene that encodes this enzyme. Generally, people with BChE deficiency do not show symptoms until they receive drugs such as mivacurium or succinylcholine.

If you have a family history of BChE deficiency or a family member who has experienced problems related to anesthesia, it is essential to inform your doctor before undergoing any procedure that requires anesthesia.

TRADE NAMES

Mivacron® (mivacurium)
Anectine® (succinylcholine)
Quelicin® (succinylcholine)
632.technical_description:
Pseudocholinesterase is a plasma enzyme produced in the liver that metabolizes anesthetic drugs such as succinylcholine and mivacurium, as well as ester-type local anesthetics such as cocaine.

Genetically, BChE deficiency is an autosomal recessive trait found in 1 in 100,000 people of European descent and varying across populations. The incidence in Jewish communities is approximately 1 in 400 individuals.

Some variants of the BCHE gene result in reduced enzymatic activity and others in a decrease in the amount of circulating active enzyme. In people with a normal functional enzyme, these drugs are rapidly metabolized, resulting in a short action (less than 10 minutes for succinylcholine). However, if there is BChE deficiency, the drug takes longer to be metabolized and eliminated from the body, prolonging neuromuscular paralysis.

The severity of this adverse effect depends on the genotype; carriers of one copy of a heterozygous BCHE variant exhibit moderate block, while carriers of two copies or homozygotes may experience muscle paralysis for more than 2 hours.

The most common variant in Caucasians is the atypical (A) variant rs1799807 (c.293A>G, p.Asp70Gly) followed by the Kalow (K) variant rs1803274 (c.1699G>A, p. Ala539Tyr). Less common variants of the BCHE gene include three fluoride-resistant or F variants such as rs28933389 (c.812C>T, p.Thr271Met), rs28933390 (c.1253G>T, p.Gly418Val) and rs121918557 (c.1072T>A, p.Leu358Ile) and silent variants called S variants. Some S variants lead to drastic reductions in BCHE activity such as rs104893684 (c.1004T>C, p.Leu335Pro).

It is important to consider that BChE deficiency may be acquired and not due to genetic alterations. Prolongation of neuromuscular block due to pseudocholinesterase deficiency may be influenced by other factors such as increasing age, pregnancy, severe renal or hepatic disease, cancer, burn injuries, malnutrition, and drug interactions.
632.technical_citations:
Alvarellos ML, McDonagh EM, Patel S, et al. PharmGKB summary: succinylcholine pathway, pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics. Pharmacogenet Genomics. 2015 Dec;25(12):622-30. 
Trujillo R, West WP. Pseudocholinesterase Deficiency. 2023 Jul 10. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–. 
Wichmann S, Færk G, Bundgaard JR, Gätke MR. Patients with prolonged effect of succinylcholine or mivacurium had novel mutations in the butyrylcholinesterase gene. Pharmacogenet Genomics. 2016 Jul;26(7):351-6.
 Zhu GD, Dawson E, Huskey A, et al. Genetic Testing for BCHE Variants Identifies Patients at Risk of Prolonged Neuromuscular Blockade in Response to Succinylcholine. Pharmgenomics Pers Med. 2020 Sep 30;13:405-414.

453.name: Methotrexate (Adverse Reactions)
453.description: Methotrexate is a structural analog of folic acid widely used in the treatment of some types of cancer and rheumatic diseases, such as rheumatoid arthritis. The presence of adverse effects is relatively common, with the MTHFR gene being one of the most studied, directly involved in the metabolism of methotrexate and folic acid.
453.overview:
Methotrexate is a drug pertaining to the antimetabolites family of drugs that presents several indications. It is used to treat rheumatoid arthritis, severe psoriasis, certain types of cancer (lymphomas, leukemias, uterus, breast, lung, head and neck) and is also used as an immunosuppressant in organ transplants to prevent rejection.
